teens.explanation: 2 Kings 9:18-20 — Both messengers asked "Is it peace?" and Jehu answered "What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me," and both joined him and did not return.

teens.question: What prophecy was fulfilled when Jehu killed Joram with an arrow?
teens.option_a: The blood of Naboth the Jezreelite and the blood of the servants of the LORD was avenged in the portion of Jezreel
teens.option_b: The word Elijah spoke at the entrance to Jezreel was fulfilled
teens.option_c: Elisha's prophecy that Joram would die in battle against Syria was fulfilled
teens.option_d: The judgment God spoke to Moses about the house of Jeroboam came upon Ahab's house
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 9:26 — "Surely I have seen yesterday the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons, saith the LORD; and I will requite thee in this plat, saith the LORD."

teens.question: What did Jezebel do when she heard Jehu had come to Jezreel, before being thrown from the window?
teens.option_a: She painted her face, tired her head, and looked out at a window
teens.option_b: She put on armour and prepared the palace guard for battle
teens.option_c: She prayed to Baal for protection and fled to an inner chamber
teens.option_d: She sent a message to Jehu offering to make peace
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 9:30 — "And when Jehu was come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she painted her face, and tired her head, and looked out at a window."

teens.question: What did Jezebel call Jehu as he approached, in reference to another treacherous official from Israel's history?
teens.option_a: Zimri, thou murderer of his master
teens.option_b: Omri's son, thou rebel against kings
teens.option_c: Joram's assassin, thou man of blood
teens.option_d: Ahab's enemy, thou troubler of Israel
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 9:31 — "And as Jehu entered in at the gate, she said, Had Zimri peace, who slew his master?"

adults.question: What were the exact words of the anointing oracle delivered by the young prophet that specified the divine mandate for Jehu's kingship?
adults.option_a: Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I have anointed thee king over the people of the LORD, even over Israel, and thou shalt smite the house of Ahab thy master, that I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets and the blood of all the servants of the LORD
adults.option_b: I anoint thee to rule Israel and to make peace with Syria and to restore the land
adults.option_c: The LORD has chosen thee from all the captains to reign in righteousness over Jacob
adults.option_d: Be king over my people Israel and walk in the way of David and thou shalt prosper
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:6-7 — "Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I have anointed thee king over the people of the LORD, even over Israel. And thou shalt smite the house of Ahab thy master, that I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets."

adults.question: How did Jehu's fellow officers respond when he told them what the young prophet had said, and what did they do with their garments?
adults.option_a: They hasted and took every man his garment and put it under Jehu on the bare stairs and blew with trumpets saying Jehu is king
adults.option_b: They were silent and waited three days before declaring loyalty to Jehu
adults.option_c: They were divided — half followed Jehu and half remained loyal to Joram
adults.option_d: They fell on their faces before Jehu and swore an oath of silence about the anointing
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:13 — "Then they hasted, and took every man his garment, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew with trumpets, saying, Jehu is king."

adults.question: What was the precise manner of Joram's death and the theological interpretation Jehu attached to the location where Joram's body was cast?
adults.option_a: Jehu drew his bow and smote Joram between his arms and the arrow went out at his heart; Jehu commanded the body to be cast in the field of Naboth as fulfillment of the LORD's oracle about Naboth's blood
adults.option_b: Joram was stabbed at the city gate and his body was cast into the dungeon
adults.option_c: Jehu called fire from heaven that consumed Joram as Elijah had done to the captains
adults.option_d: Joram fell from his chariot and Jehu's servants completed the execution with swords
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:24,26 — "And Jehu drew a bow with his full strength, and smote Jehoram between his arms, and the arrow went out at his heart... cast him in the portion of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite."

adults.question: What question did Jehu pose to Bidkar his captain about the prophecy of Naboth, and which prophet's oracle did he invoke?
adults.option_a: He said remember how Elijah spoke burden against Ahab, and God said he would requite Ahab in Naboth's plat for the blood of Naboth and his sons
adults.option_b: He invoked Moses' law of blood vengeance as justification for killing Joram
adults.option_c: He cited the oracle of Micaiah who had predicted Ahab's death in battle
adults.option_d: He quoted the writing of Jehu son of Hanani against the house of Baasha as precedent
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:25-26 — "Then said Jehu to Bidkar his captain, Take up, and cast him in the portion of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite: for remember how that, when I and thou rode together after Ahab his father, the LORD laid this burden upon him."

adults.question: How did Ahaziah king of Judah die in the account, and where was he buried?
adults.option_a: He was wounded by Jehu's men in the going up to Gur by Ibleam, fled to Megiddo and died there, and was buried in Jerusalem in the sepulchre of his fathers
adults.option_b: He surrendered to Jehu at Samaria and was executed in the palace courtyard
adults.option_c: He fled to Egypt and died there of his wounds never to return
adults.option_d: He was killed by the arrow that also wounded Joram at the vineyard of Naboth
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:27-28 — "But when Ahaziah the king of Judah saw this, he fled by the way of the garden house. And Jehu followed after him, and said, Smite him also in the chariot... and he died there. And his servants carried him... to Jerusalem, and buried him in his sepulchre."

adults.question: What does Jezebel's application of cosmetics and her taunt at the window suggest about her character and strategy in her final moments?
adults.option_a: She died as she lived — defiant, theatrically powerful, and attempting to manipulate by evoking the fate of Zimri to warn Jehu that assassins of kings never prosper
adults.option_b: She was repenting of her sins and beautifying herself as a sign of submission
adults.option_c: She was expecting Jehu to spare her because of her royal status as queen mother
adults.option_d: She was attempting to charm Jehu romantically to convince him not to kill her
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:30-31 — "She painted her face, and tired her head, and looked out at a window. And as Jehu entered in at the gate, she said, Had Zimri peace, who slew his master?"

adults.question: What was left of Jezebel's body when the men went to bury her, and what did this fulfill precisely?
adults.option_a: They found no more of her than the skull, feet, and palms of her hands — fulfilling Elijah's word that dogs shall eat Jezebel in the portion of Jezreel
adults.option_b: Her body was completely consumed so there was nothing left to bury
adults.option_c: Only her garments remained as the dogs had eaten everything else
adults.option_d: Her body was intact but unrecognisable after being trampled by horses
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:35-36 — "And they went to bury her: but they found no more of her than the skull, and the feet, and the palms of her hands... This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying, In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel."

adults.question: How does the narrative of Jehu's revolution in chapter 9 demonstrate the Deuteronomistic theology that prophetic words against evil dynasties are always fulfilled?
adults.option_a: Every element — Ahab's blood at Naboth's field, Jezebel's consumption by dogs, Joram's death — is explicitly linked to earlier oracles of Elijah, showing the inevitability of divine judgment
adults.option_b: The chapter shows that military coups are sometimes approved by God
adults.option_c: The narrative proves that the northern kingdom was always under God's blessing
adults.option_d: The chapter teaches that prophets who anoint kings will be rewarded with political power
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 9:26,36 — Both Joram's death in Naboth's field and Jezebel's being eaten by dogs are explicitly tied back to Elijah's oracles, the narrator noting each time "This is the word of the LORD which he spake."

kids.question: Jehu sent letters demanding that the elders of Samaria choose a new king from among Ahab's sons. What did the elders do instead?
kids.option_a: They surrendered to Jehu and said they would do whatever he commanded
kids.option_b: They chose the oldest son of Ahab as their new king
kids.option_c: They sent an army against Jehu
kids.option_d: They asked the king of Syria for help
kids.explanation: 2 Kings 10:5 — "And he that was over the house, and he that was over the city, the elders also, and the bringers up of the children, sent to Jehu, saying, We are thy servants."

kids.question: What was sent to Jehu in baskets from Samaria after Ahab's seventy sons were killed?
kids.option_a: The heads of Ahab's seventy sons
kids.option_c: The seals and signet rings of Ahab's family
kids.option_d: Silver and gold from the palace
kids.explanation: 2 Kings 10:7-8 — "And it came to pass, when the letter came to them, that they took the king's sons, and slew seventy persons, and put their heads in baskets, and sent him them to Jezreel."

kids.question: Jehu gathered all the worshippers of Baal into the temple for a special feast. What did he do to them?
kids.option_a: He killed them all in the temple
kids.option_b: He made them bow down to the LORD
kids.option_c: He sent them out of the land as exiles
kids.explanation: 2 Kings 10:25 — "And it came to pass, as soon as he had made an end of offering the burnt offering, that Jehu said to the guard and to the captains, Go in, and slay them; let none come forth. And they smote them with the edge of the sword."

teens.question: What test did Jehu use to determine whether the elders of Samaria were truly loyal to him?
teens.option_a: He asked them to take the heads of Ahab's sons and bring them to him by the next day at Jezreel
teens.option_b: He commanded them to pay tribute of silver and gold within three days
teens.option_c: He had them swear an oath on the altar of the LORD
teens.option_d: He sent them to fight the Syrians as a test of their military loyalty
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 10:6 — "Then he wrote a letter the second time to them, saying, If ye be mine, and if ye will hearken unto my voice, take ye the heads of the men your master's sons, and come to me to Jezreel by to morrow this time."

teens.question: How did Jehu justify the killing of Ahab's household to the people of Jezreel after displaying the severed heads?
teens.option_a: He said the LORD had fulfilled all He spoke against the house of Ahab and that he only killed Joram — the rest had been done by others in fulfillment of God's word
teens.option_b: He declared himself the righteous avenger of Naboth's blood
teens.option_c: He quoted the law of Moses regarding the punishment of idolatrous kings
teens.option_d: He said the elders and people of Samaria were equally responsible for all the deaths
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 10:9-10 — "And he said to all the people... Know now that there shall fall unto the earth nothing of the word of the LORD, which the LORD spake concerning the house of Ahab."

teens.question: Who were the forty-two men Jehu killed on the road to Samaria when he met them, and what had they come for?
teens.option_a: The brethren of Ahaziah king of Judah who had come down to greet the children of the king and the children of the queen
teens.option_b: Forty-two priests of Baal travelling from Jezreel to Samaria
teens.option_c: Ahab's former military commanders returning from Ramoth-gilead
teens.option_d: Syrian traders who were carrying tribute to the house of Ahab
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 10:13-14 — "Jehu met with the brethren of Ahaziah king of Judah... And he said, Take them alive. And they took them alive, and slew them at the pit of the shearing house, even two and forty men."

teens.question: What deceptive proclamation did Jehu make to gather all the Baal worshippers together?
teens.option_a: He declared a solemn assembly for Baal, saying Ahab served Baal a little but Jehu shall serve him much
teens.option_b: He announced a great feast in Baal's honour for all the prophets of Baal
teens.option_c: He sent messages that Baal had appeared to him in a vision commanding a national festival
teens.option_d: He offered prizes of silver and gold to all who attended the temple of Baal
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 10:18-19 — "And Jehu gathered all the people together, and said unto them, Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu shall serve him much."

teens.question: What was the spiritual failure of Jehu noted at the end of chapter 10 despite all his actions against the house of Ahab and Baal worship?
teens.option_a: He departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat who made Israel to sin, keeping the golden calves at Bethel and Dan
teens.option_b: He failed to tear down the high places throughout Israel
teens.option_c: He married a daughter of a Baal worshipper against the law of Moses
teens.option_d: He did not restore the temple in Jerusalem as David had commanded
teens.explanation: 2 Kings 10:29 — "Howbeit from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, Jehu departed not from after them, to wit, the golden calves that were in Bethel, and that were in Dan."

adults.question: What was the rhetorical strategy of Jehu's first letter to the elders of Samaria, and how did the elders' complete capitulation serve his political purposes?
adults.option_a: By proposing they crown one of Ahab's seventy sons and fight for him, Jehu forced the elders to choose between armed resistance and submission — their surrender proved they would also execute Ahab's sons on command
adults.option_b: Jehu offered generous terms of peace to the elders to win their loyalty before marching on them
adults.option_c: He threatened to destroy Samaria immediately unless they surrendered within one day
adults.option_d: He promised to spare the seventy sons if the elders paid a ransom of silver and gold
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:1-5 — Jehu's letter challenged them to fight; their terrified reply — "We are thy servants, and will do all that thou shalt bid us" — set up the second letter demanding the heads.

adults.question: How many members of Ahab's extended household, allies, acquaintances, and priests did Jehu kill in Jezreel, and what theological verdict does the narrator attach to this?
adults.option_a: He slew all that remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel, all his great men and his acquaintance and his priests, until he left him none remaining, fulfilling Elijah's prophecy
adults.option_b: He killed exactly seventy sons and spared the household servants
adults.option_c: He killed three hundred men of war from Ahab's household but let the priests flee
adults.option_d: He killed everyone in Jezreel except the family members who converted to serve the LORD
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:11 — "So Jehu slew all that remained of the house of Ahab in Jezreel, and all his great men, and his kinsfolks, and his priests, until he left him none remaining."

adults.question: Who was Jehonadab the son of Rechab, and what role did he play in Jehu's reform program?
adults.option_a: He was invited by Jehu to witness that Jehu was zealous for the LORD, and he joined Jehu in the chariot before the destruction of Baal worship
adults.option_b: He was Jehu's chief priest who performed the burnt offering in the Baal temple
adults.option_c: He was a military captain who commanded the guard during the Baal temple massacre
adults.option_d: He was the scribe who recorded Jehu's deeds in the chronicles of Israel
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:15-16 — "And when he was departed thence, he lighted on Jehonadab the son of Rechab coming to meet him... and he took him up to him into the chariot. And he said, Come with me, and see my zeal for the LORD."

adults.question: What specific security measures did Jehu implement to ensure no Baal worshippers escaped the temple massacre?
adults.option_a: He posted eighty men outside telling them any man who escaped would be killed in exchange for his own life, and before killing the worshippers he verified no servants of the LORD were present
adults.option_b: He locked the temple doors and set fire to the building from outside
adults.option_c: He ordered the temple surrounded by a thousand soldiers with drawn swords
adults.option_d: He gave the guard poison to add to the feast food before the assembly arrived
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:23-24 — "And Jehu went, and Jehonadab the son of Rechab, into the house of Baal... And he said unto him that was over the vestry, Bring forth vestments for all the worshippers of Baal. And he appointed fourscore men without."

adults.question: What was done to the temple of Baal after the massacre of its worshippers, and what was the land used for afterward?
adults.option_a: They brought forth the images out of the house of Baal and burned them, broke down the house of Baal, and made it a draught house unto this day
adults.option_b: They converted the temple into a house of worship for the LORD
adults.option_c: They pulled down the walls but preserved the site as a memorial of judgment
adults.option_d: They buried the bodies of the slain Baal worshippers in the temple courts
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:26-27 — "And they brought forth the images out of the house of Baal, and burned them. And they brake down the image of Baal, and brake down the house of Baal, and made it a draught house unto this day."

adults.question: What divine promise did God make to Jehu because of his obedience in executing judgment on the house of Ahab?
adults.option_a: Thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel
adults.option_b: Thou shalt be remembered as the greatest king of Israel for ever
adults.option_c: I will give thee peace all the days of thy life and no enemy shall prevail
adults.option_d: All the land of Syria shall be given to thy children as inheritance
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:30 — "And the LORD said unto Jehu, Because thou hast done well in executing that which is right in mine eyes, and hast done unto the house of Ahab according to all that was in mine heart, thy children of the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel."

adults.question: What territorial losses did Israel suffer during Jehu's reign due to his spiritual failures, and at whose hands?
adults.option_a: Hazael smote Israel in all the coasts of Israel from Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead — the Gadites, Reubenites, Manassites — from Aroer by Arnon, even Gilead and Bashan
adults.option_b: The Philistines retook all the coastal cities Israel had held since David
adults.option_c: Assyria invaded and took the northern tribes into captivity during Jehu's reign
adults.option_d: Egypt invaded and took the Negev and the land of Judah as far as Jerusalem
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:32-33 — "In those days the LORD began to cut Israel short: and Hazael smote them in all the coasts of Israel; From Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead."

adults.question: How does the theological assessment of Jehu's reign demonstrate the complexity of divine judgment in the Deuteronomistic History — where partial obedience brings partial reward?
adults.option_a: Jehu received a dynastic promise of four generations for destroying Baalism, yet suffered territorial loss because he kept Jeroboam's calves, showing God rewards specific obedience while still judging specific disobedience
adults.option_b: Jehu's reign shows that military success always accompanies faithfulness to God
adults.option_c: The account teaches that destroying idol worship is the only criterion by which kings are judged
adults.option_d: Jehu is presented as fully righteous because he executed more of God's word than any previous king
adults.explanation: 2 Kings 10:30-33 — God praised and rewarded Jehu's anti-Ahab revolution (v.30) while simultaneously cutting Israel short through Hazael for Jehu's persistence in Jeroboam's sins (vv.31-33), illustrating graduated divine accountability.

kids.question: Who tried to kill all the royal children and became queen?
kids.explanation: When Athaliah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the seed royal (2 Kings 11:1).

kids.question: Who hid the baby Joash in the temple for six years?
kids.explanation: Jehosheba took Joash and hid him in the house of the LORD for six years (2 Kings 11:3).

kids.question: What happened to Queen Athaliah at the end of the chapter?
kids.option_c: She repented and became a prophetess
kids.explanation: They laid hands on her; and she went by the way by the which the horses came into the king's house: and there was she slain (2 Kings 11:16).

teens.question: How old was Joash when he was crowned king by Jehoiada the priest?
teens.option_a: Seven years old
teens.option_b: Twelve years old
teens.option_c: Five years old
teens.option_d: Ten years old
teens.explanation: Joash was seven years old when he began to reign (2 Kings 11:21).

teens.question: Who organised the coup that overthrew Athaliah and crowned Joash?
teens.option_a: Jehoiada the priest
teens.option_b: The army commander
teens.option_c: The prophet Elisha
teens.option_d: The tribe of Levi
teens.explanation: Jehoiada the priest commanded the captains of the hundreds to bring Joash out, and they made him king (2 Kings 11:9-12).

teens.question: What was placed in Joash's hand at his coronation?
teens.option_a: The testimony
teens.option_b: A golden sceptre
teens.option_c: The ark of the covenant
teens.option_d: A sword
teens.explanation: He brought forth the king's son, and put the crown upon him, and gave him the testimony; and they made him king (2 Kings 11:12).

teens.question: What covenant did Jehoiada make on the day of the coronation?
teens.option_a: A covenant between the LORD, the king, and the people
teens.option_b: A covenant of peace with the surrounding nations
teens.option_c: A covenant to rebuild the temple
teens.option_d: A covenant to destroy all idols in Israel
teens.explanation: Jehoiada made a covenant between the LORD and the king and the people, that they should be the LORD's people (2 Kings 11:17).

teens.question: What did the people do to the temple of Baal after Athaliah's overthrow?
teens.option_a: They broke it down and slew its priest Mattan
teens.option_b: They converted it into a store house
teens.option_c: They sealed it shut
teens.option_d: They burned it to the ground
teens.explanation: All the people went into the house of Baal, and brake it down; his altars and his images brake they in pieces thoroughly, and slew Mattan the priest of Baal (2 Kings 11:18).

adults.question: Who was Jehosheba, the woman who hid young Joash?
adults.option_a: The daughter of King Joram and sister of Ahaziah
adults.option_b: The wife of the high priest and a prophetess
adults.option_c: A Levite nurse appointed by Jehoiada
adults.option_d: The mother of Jehoiada
adults.explanation: Jehosheba, the daughter of king Joram, sister of Ahaziah, took Joash the son of Ahaziah (2 Kings 11:2).

adults.question: How long was Athaliah's reign before she was overthrown?
adults.option_a: Six years
adults.option_b: Seven years
adults.option_c: Four years
adults.option_d: Ten years
adults.explanation: Athaliah did reign over the land for six years while Joash was hidden (2 Kings 11:3).

adults.question: What instructions did Jehoiada give regarding the guards' Sabbath rotations?
adults.option_a: Two parts to keep the king's house and one part at the gate Sur
adults.option_b: One third to guard the temple gate, one third at the palace gate, one third at the gate behind the guard
adults.option_c: All guards to assemble at the temple at the third hour
adults.option_d: The incoming guard to surround Joash and the outgoing to stand at the wall
adults.explanation: Jehoiada commanded the captains: a third part...shall keep the watch of the king's house...and a third part at the gate Sur (2 Kings 11:5-6).

adults.question: What specific command was given to anyone who followed Athaliah into the temple at the moment of the coup?
adults.option_a: He that followeth her, kill with the sword
adults.option_b: He that entereth the temple shall be bound
adults.option_c: Seize him but do not kill him inside the holy place
adults.option_d: Warn him once before striking him down
adults.explanation: He that followeth her kill with the sword. For the priest had said, Let her not be slain in the house of the LORD (2 Kings 11:15).

adults.question: What did Athaliah cry out when she saw Joash crowned?
adults.option_a: Treason, Treason
adults.option_b: God forbid, God forbid
adults.option_c: Death to the usurper
adults.option_d: Where is my army
adults.explanation: When Athaliah heard the noise of the guard and of the people, she came to the people into the temple of the LORD. And when she looked, behold, the king stood by a pillar, as the manner was, and she rent her clothes, and cried, Treason, Treason (2 Kings 11:13-14).

adults.question: Through what gate was Athaliah taken out to be executed?
adults.option_a: The horse gate
adults.option_b: The water gate
adults.option_c: The sheep gate
adults.option_d: The valley gate
adults.explanation: She went by the way by the which the horses came into the king's house: and there was she slain (2 Kings 11:16).

adults.question: What was the condition of the city described after Athaliah was slain?
adults.option_a: The city was in quiet
adults.option_b: The city mourned for seven days
adults.option_c: The people shouted for a full day
adults.option_d: The city was set on fire
adults.explanation: And all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was in quiet (2 Kings 11:20).

adults.question: What theological significance does the survival of Joash represent in redemptive history?
adults.option_a: The preservation of the Davidic line through which the Messiah would come
adults.option_b: The proof that God favoured the tribe of Judah above all others
adults.option_c: The fulfilment of Elijah's prophecy against the house of Ahab
adults.option_d: The establishment of a priestly monarchy in Jerusalem
adults.explanation: God preserved the line of David — Joash was a descendant through whom the Messianic promise of 2 Samuel 7 would continue, as Athaliah's near extinction of the royal seed threatened the covenant (2 Kings 11:2-3).

kids.question: What did King Joash want to repair?
kids.explanation: Joash said to the priests, All the money...let them repair the breaches of the house (2 Kings 12:5).

kids.question: What did Jehoiada the priest make to collect money for the temple repairs?
kids.option_a: A chest with a hole in the lid
kids.option_b: A golden bowl at the altar
kids.option_c: A large basket at the gate
kids.option_d: A treasury room in the temple
kids.explanation: Jehoiada the priest took a chest, and bored a hole in the lid of it, and set it beside the altar (2 Kings 12:9).

kids.question: How did King Joash die?
kids.option_a: He was killed by his own servants
kids.option_b: He died of old age in his palace
kids.option_c: He was killed in battle with Assyria
kids.explanation: His servants arose, and made a conspiracy, and slew Joash in the house of Millo (2 Kings 12:20).

teens.question: How old was Joash when he became king and how long did he reign in Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Seven years old; forty years
teens.option_b: Ten years old; thirty years
teens.option_c: Five years old; twenty years
teens.option_d: Twelve years old; fifteen years
teens.explanation: Jehoash was seven years old when he began to reign. In Jerusalem he reigned forty years (2 Kings 12:1).

teens.question: What did Jehoash instruct the priests to do with all the money brought into the house of the LORD?
teens.option_a: Repair the breaches of the house wherever any breach shall be found
teens.option_b: Divide it equally between the priests and the temple workers
teens.option_c: Send it to the king's treasury for safekeeping
teens.option_d: Use it to buy sacrifices for the altar
teens.explanation: Let the priests take it to them, every man of his acquaintance: and let them repair the breaches of the house, wheresoever any breach shall be found (2 Kings 12:5).

teens.question: Why did King Joash rebuke the priests in the twenty-third year of his reign?
teens.option_a: They had not repaired the breaches of the house
teens.option_b: They had stolen money from the chest
teens.option_c: They had stopped offering sacrifices
teens.option_d: They had built a new altar to Baal
teens.explanation: In the three and twentieth year of king Jehoash the priests had not repaired the breaches of the house (2 Kings 12:6).

teens.question: How did Hazael king of Syria threaten Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He came up and set his face to go up to Jerusalem
teens.option_b: He sent a great army to besiege the city for three years
teens.option_c: He cut off the water supply to the city
teens.option_d: He burned the temple treasury
teens.explanation: Hazael king of Syria went up and fought against Gath, and took it: and Hazael set his face to go up to Jerusalem (2 Kings 12:17).

teens.question: How did Joash persuade Hazael not to attack Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He gave him all the hallowed things and the gold of the temple and palace treasuries
teens.option_b: He promised to pay tribute every year
teens.option_c: He surrendered two cities
teens.option_d: He sent his daughter to Hazael as a wife
teens.explanation: Jehoash took all the hallowed things...and all the gold that was found in the treasures of the house of the LORD, and in the king's house, and sent it to Hazael king of Syria: and he went away from Jerusalem (2 Kings 12:18).

adults.question: What was the overall spiritual assessment of Joash's reign despite his good deeds?
adults.option_a: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all his days, taught by Jehoiada, but the high places were not taken away
adults.option_b: He departed from the LORD in his old age and worshipped idols
adults.option_c: He was fully righteous with no fault recorded
adults.option_d: He provoked the LORD more than any king before him
adults.explanation: Jehoash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him. But the high places were not taken away (2 Kings 12:2-3).

adults.question: What reform did Jehoiada institute regarding the new collection chest?
adults.option_a: The priests that kept the door put therein all the money that was brought into the house of the LORD, and the king's scribe counted it
adults.option_b: The Levites carried the chest to every gate to collect offerings weekly
adults.option_c: Only the high priest could open the chest at the new moon
adults.option_d: The chest was sealed and opened only at the year of jubilee
adults.explanation: The priests that kept the door put therein all the money that was brought into the house of the LORD. And when they saw that there was much money in the chest...the king's scribe and the high priest...told the money (2 Kings 12:9-10).

adults.question: What does the account of the temple repairs say about the honesty of the workmen?
adults.option_a: They dealt faithfully so no reckoning was made with them
adults.option_b: They were required to submit weekly accounts to the priest
adults.option_c: Several were found dishonest and dismissed
adults.option_d: They were paid double for their honesty
adults.explanation: They reckoned not with the men, into whose hand they delivered the money to be bestowed on workmen: for they dealt faithfully (2 Kings 12:15).

adults.question: What items were NOT repaired with the collected money according to verse 13?
adults.option_a: Bowls of silver, snuffers, basons, trumpets, or any vessels of gold or silver
adults.option_b: The great bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz
adults.option_c: The altar of incense inside the temple
adults.option_d: The ark of the covenant's carrying poles
adults.explanation: Howbeit there were not made for the house of the LORD bowls of silver, snuffers, basons, trumpets, any vessels of gold, or vessels of silver, of the money that was brought into the house of the LORD (2 Kings 12:13).

adults.question: Who were the two servants of Joash who conspired against him and where was he killed?
adults.option_a: Jozachar the son of Shimeath and Jehozabad the son of Shomer; in the house of Millo going down to Silla
adults.option_b: Ahishar and Adoniram; in the palace garden
adults.option_c: Benaiah and Zadok; at the gate of the guard
adults.option_d: Two unnamed Philistine captives; in the dungeon
adults.explanation: Jozachar the son of Shimeath, and Jehozabad the son of Shomer, his servants, smote him, and he died; and they buried him with his fathers in the city of David: and Amaziah his son reigned in his stead (2 Kings 12:21).

adults.question: How was the money that passed through the priests' hands used differently from the guilt and sin offerings?
adults.option_a: The trespass money and sin offerings were the priests' own; only the other money went to the house repair
adults.option_b: The priests received none of the guilt money as it was all sent to Hazael
adults.option_c: Both types went equally to repairs
adults.option_d: The priests could only use guilt money for their own food
adults.explanation: The trespass money and sin money was not brought into the house of the LORD: it was the priests' (2 Kings 12:16).

adults.question: Where was Joash buried and who succeeded him?
adults.option_a: With his fathers in the city of David; Amaziah his son
adults.option_b: In Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jehoahaz his son
adults.option_c: Outside the city wall as a dishonoured king; Jotham
adults.option_d: In the valley of Jehoshaphat; Uzziah
adults.explanation: They buried him with his fathers in the city of David: and Amaziah his son reigned in his stead (2 Kings 12:21).

adults.question: What broader theological tension does chapter 12 reveal about Joash's reign?
adults.option_a: He was entirely dependent on Jehoiada for his faithfulness, foreshadowing his later apostasy after the priest's death as recorded in 2 Chronicles 24
adults.option_b: He proved that a godly king alone can maintain the covenant without priests
adults.option_c: He showed that material prosperity always follows temple maintenance
adults.option_d: He demonstrated that political alliances are acceptable when the nation is threatened
adults.explanation: Joash did right all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him (2 Kings 12:2), and 2 Chronicles 24:17-18 records that after Jehoiada died, Joash abandoned the house of the LORD — showing his faithfulness was not self-sustained.

kids.question: What amazing thing happened when a dead man touched Elisha's bones?
kids.option_a: The man came back to life
kids.explanation: When the man was let down, and touched the bones of Elisha, he revived, and stood up on his feet (2 Kings 13:21).

kids.question: Who was the king of Israel when Elisha died?
kids.explanation: Now Elisha was fallen sick of his sickness whereof he died. And Joash the king of Israel came down unto him (2 Kings 13:14).

kids.question: What did Elisha tell Joash to take and strike the ground with?
kids.explanation: Elisha said, Take the arrows. And he took them. And he said unto the king of Israel, Smite upon the ground (2 Kings 13:18).

teens.question: Who was the father of Joash (Jehoash) king of Israel?
teens.option_a: Jehoahaz
teens.option_b: Jehu
teens.option_c: Jeroboam
teens.option_d: Ahab
teens.explanation: In the thirty and seventh year of Joash king of Judah began Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz to reign over Israel (2 Kings 13:10).

teens.question: What did Elisha tell Joash the arrows he shot eastward represented?
teens.option_a: The arrow of the LORD's deliverance and the arrow of deliverance from Syria
teens.option_b: Victory over all surrounding nations
teens.option_c: The destruction of the house of Ahab
teens.option_d: The restoration of all Israel's lost territories
teens.explanation: Elisha said, The arrow of the LORD's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance from Syria: for thou shalt smite the Syrians in Aphek, till thou have consumed them (2 Kings 13:17).

teens.question: How many times did Joash strike the ground with the arrows, and why was Elisha angry?
teens.option_a: Three times; he should have struck five or six times
teens.option_b: Five times; he should have struck seven times
teens.option_c: Two times; he should have struck at least four
teens.option_d: Four times; he should have struck only once
teens.explanation: He smote thrice, and stayed. And the man of God was wroth with him, and said, Thou shouldest have smitten five or six times (2 Kings 13:18-19).

teens.question: What title did Joash king of Israel call Elisha when he was dying?
teens.option_a: My father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof
teens.option_b: Prophet of the LORD, servant of God
teens.option_c: The man of God and holy one of Israel
teens.option_d: Teacher, seer, and lamp of Judah
teens.explanation: Joash came down unto him, and wept over his face, and said, O my father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof (2 Kings 13:14).

teens.question: Despite the wickedness of Israel, why did God show compassion and not destroy them in Jehoahaz's time?
teens.option_a: Because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
teens.option_b: Because Jehoahaz cried to the LORD and God heard his prayer
teens.option_c: Because Elisha interceded for the nation
teens.option_d: Because the people repented at the preaching of a prophet
teens.explanation: The LORD was gracious unto them, and had compassion on them, and had respect unto them, because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (2 Kings 13:23).

adults.question: How many horsemen and chariots were left to Jehoahaz by the end of Syrian oppression?
adults.option_a: Fifty horsemen, ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen
adults.option_b: A hundred horsemen, fifty chariots, and fifty thousand footmen
adults.option_c: No cavalry at all; only foot soldiers remained
adults.option_d: Two hundred horsemen and twenty chariots
adults.explanation: There was not left to Jehoahaz but fifty horsemen, and ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen; for the king of Syria had destroyed them, and had made them like the dust by threshing (2 Kings 13:7).

adults.question: What deliverer did God provide for Israel during Jehoahaz's reign in response to his supplication?
adults.option_a: A saviour so that they went out from under the hand of the Syrians
adults.option_b: Elisha who performed a miracle that routed the Syrian army
adults.option_c: A coalition with Judah that drove out Syria
adults.option_d: An angel who destroyed the Syrian camp
adults.explanation: The LORD gave Israel a saviour, so that they went out from under the hand of the Syrians (2 Kings 13:5).

adults.question: What was the sin of Jehoahaz that is specifically named?
adults.option_a: He walked in the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin; he departed not therefrom
adults.option_b: He sacrificed his children to Molech
adults.option_c: He made an alliance with the king of Syria
adults.option_d: He removed the Levites from the temple service
adults.explanation: He walked in the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which made Israel to sin; he departed not therefrom (2 Kings 13:2).

adults.question: What remained in Samaria as part of the degraded state of Israel's worship?
adults.option_a: There remained the grove also in Samaria
adults.option_b: The golden calves set up by Jeroboam at Dan and Bethel
adults.option_c: An Asherah pole installed by Jezebel in the temple
adults.option_d: A high place dedicated to Chemosh
adults.explanation: There remained the grove also in Samaria (2 Kings 13:6).

adults.question: What does the miraculous revival at Elisha's tomb foreshadow theologically?
adults.option_a: The resurrection power of God that would ultimately be fully revealed in Christ's own resurrection
adults.option_b: That Israel would be spiritually revived in the last days
adults.option_c: That Elisha was greater than Elijah
adults.option_d: That touching holy objects brings life
adults.explanation: The dead man revived and stood up on his feet upon touching Elisha's bones (2 Kings 13:21), prefiguring the life-giving power that would be perfectly displayed in the resurrection of Christ (cf. John 11:25).

adults.question: How many times did Joash defeat Syria, consistent with the three strikes of the arrows?
adults.option_a: Three times
adults.option_b: Five times
adults.option_c: Once decisively
adults.option_d: Seven times
adults.explanation: Joash the son of Jehoahaz took again out of the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael the cities...Three times did Joash beat him (2 Kings 13:25).

adults.question: What does the phrase "saviour" (Hebrew moshia) in verse 5 recall about the pattern of Israel's history?
adults.option_a: It echoes the language of the judges period where God raised deliverers for an oppressed and repentant people
adults.option_b: It refers specifically to Elisha who was a type of Christ
adults.option_c: It points forward to the Messianic king from David's line
adults.option_d: It indicates a priestly deliverer like Samuel
adults.explanation: The LORD gave Israel a saviour (2 Kings 13:5), using the same term (moshia) found throughout Judges for God's cycle of judgment, repentance, and deliverance in the history of Israel.

adults.question: What is the significance of God's covenant remembrance with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in this context?
adults.option_a: It shows that national preservation did not depend on Israel's righteousness but on God's unconditional patriarchal promises
adults.option_b: It proves that the Mosaic covenant had been entirely replaced by the Abrahamic
adults.option_c: It means Israel could sin without consequence because of the covenant
adults.option_d: It was invoked by Moses at Sinai but has no bearing on later kings
adults.explanation: The LORD had compassion on them because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (2 Kings 13:23), showing that God's preservation of sinful Israel rested on His sovereign promise, not their merit.

kids.question: What nation did King Amaziah of Judah defeat in the valley of salt?
kids.explanation: He slew of Edom in the valley of salt ten thousand, and took Selah by war (2 Kings 14:7).

kids.question: What did Amaziah do to the servants who killed his father Joash?
kids.explanation: He slew his servants which had slain the king his father (2 Kings 14:5).

kids.question: Who restored the border of Israel from the entering of Hamath to the sea of the plain?
kids.explanation: He restored the coast of Israel from the entering of Hamath unto the sea of the plain, according to the word of the LORD God of Israel (2 Kings 14:25).

teens.question: What was the condition placed on Amaziah's execution of the conspirators who killed his father?
teens.option_a: He killed the servants but did not put to death the children of the murderers, according to the law of Moses
teens.option_b: He killed them only after consulting the high priest
teens.option_c: He waited until he had reigned ten years before acting
teens.option_d: He spared them all because he feared a revolt
teens.explanation: The children of the murderers he slew not: according unto that which is written in the book of the law of Moses (2 Kings 14:6).

teens.question: After his victory over Edom, Amaziah challenged which king to battle?
teens.option_a: Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz king of Israel
teens.option_b: Hazael king of Syria
teens.option_c: Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria
teens.option_d: Mesha king of Moab
teens.explanation: Amaziah sent messengers to Jehoash, the son of Jehoahaz son of Jehu, king of Israel, saying, Come, let us look one another in the face (2 Kings 14:8).

teens.question: What parable did Jehoash use to warn Amaziah not to attack him?
teens.option_a: The thistle of Lebanon asking the cedar for his daughter in marriage, then trampled by a wild beast
teens.option_b: A weak vine trying to climb a mighty oak and being thrown down
teens.option_c: A sparrow boasting against an eagle and being devoured
teens.option_d: A small spring trying to become a great river but drying up
teens.explanation: Jehoash said to Amaziah, The thistle that was in Lebanon sent to the cedar...And there passed by a wild beast in Lebanon, and trode down the thistle (2 Kings 14:9).

teens.question: What was the result of the battle between Amaziah and Jehoash of Israel at Beth-shemesh?
teens.option_a: Judah was put to the worse, Amaziah was taken captive, Jerusalem's wall was broken down
teens.option_b: Israel was defeated and Jehoash was killed
teens.option_c: The battle was a draw and both kings agreed to peace
teens.option_d: Amaziah won a great victory and took much spoil
teens.explanation: Judah was put to the worse before Israel; and they fled every man to their tents. And Jehoash king of Israel took Amaziah king of Judah...at Beth-shemesh (2 Kings 14:12-13).

teens.question: How long did Jeroboam II reign over Israel in Samaria?
teens.option_a: Forty and one years
teens.option_b: Twenty and five years
teens.option_c: Thirty years
teens.option_d: Fifty years
teens.explanation: Jeroboam...reigned forty and one years in Samaria (2 Kings 14:23).

adults.question: What was the spiritual assessment of Amaziah's reign?
adults.option_a: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not like David his father; he did according to all things as Joash his father did
adults.option_b: He was fully righteous and removed all the high places
adults.option_c: He walked in the ways of the kings of Israel making Israel to sin
adults.option_d: He was the most wicked king Judah had ever seen
adults.explanation: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not like David his father: he did according to all things as Joash his father did (2 Kings 14:3).

adults.question: What specific Mosaic law did Amaziah cite in not killing the children of the assassins?
adults.option_a: The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, nor the children be put to death for the fathers
adults.option_b: Every man shall be put to death for his own sin only
adults.option_c: The children of murderers shall be enslaved but not killed
adults.option_d: Only the firstborn son of a murderer shall bear his father's guilt
adults.explanation: According to that which is written in the book of the law of Moses, wherein the LORD commanded, saying, The fathers shall not be put to death for the children (2 Kings 14:6; cf. Deuteronomy 24:16).

adults.question: How much gold, silver, and goods did Jehoash of Israel take from Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: All the gold and silver and all the vessels found in the house of the LORD and in the king's house, and hostages
adults.option_b: Only the temple treasury and left the palace untouched
adults.option_c: Ten talents of gold and a hundred talents of silver
adults.option_d: The ark and the golden altar
adults.explanation: He took all the gold and silver, and all the vessels that were found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house...and returned to Samaria (2 Kings 14:14).

adults.question: Through which prophet was the prophecy given concerning Jeroboam II's restoration of Israel's borders?
adults.option_a: Jonah the son of Amittai the prophet, which was of Gath-hepher
adults.option_b: Amos the herdsman of Tekoa
adults.option_c: Hosea the son of Beeri
adults.option_d: Micah the Morasthite
adults.explanation: According to the word of the LORD God of Israel, which he spake by the hand of his servant Jonah, the son of Amittai, the prophet, which was of Gath-hepher (2 Kings 14:25).

adults.question: What was the theological reason God allowed Jeroboam II to restore Israel's territory despite his wickedness?
adults.option_a: The LORD saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter...and had not said he would blot out the name of Israel
adults.option_b: Jeroboam II had repented late in his reign
adults.option_c: God was rewarding Israel for the faithfulness of Elisha
adults.option_d: There was no righteous king available so God used the wicked one
adults.explanation: The LORD saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter: for there was not any shut up, nor any left, nor any helper for Israel. And the LORD said not that he would blot out the name of Israel (2 Kings 14:26-27).

adults.question: Where was Amaziah eventually killed and how did he die?
adults.option_a: He fled to Lachish, where a conspiracy was sent after him and he was slain there
adults.option_b: He died peacefully in Jerusalem of old age
adults.option_c: He was killed in battle against the Philistines
adults.option_d: He was executed by his own guard for apostasy
adults.explanation: They made a conspiracy against him in Jerusalem: and he fled to Lachish; but they sent after him to Lachish, and slew him there (2 Kings 14:19).

adults.question: How much of Jerusalem's wall did Jehoash of Israel demolish?
adults.option_a: Four hundred cubits, from the gate of Ephraim unto the corner gate
adults.option_b: Half the northern wall
adults.option_c: The entire south wall facing Judah
adults.option_d: From the fish gate to the valley gate
adults.explanation: Jehoash brake down the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim unto the corner gate, four hundred cubits (2 Kings 14:13).

adults.question: What does the career of Jeroboam II reveal about the relationship between political power and spiritual fidelity in the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: God can use even wicked rulers to fulfil His redemptive purposes for His people without endorsing their sin
adults.option_b: Political success is always a sign of divine approval of the king's character
adults.option_c: God abandoned Israel during this period and Jeroboam succeeded by his own military skill
adults.option_d: The northern kingdom repented under Jeroboam II but later fell back into sin
adults.explanation: Jeroboam II did evil in the sight of the LORD yet God used him to save Israel and restore its borders (2 Kings 14:24-27), showing that divine sovereignty operates through and despite human wickedness.

kids.question: What disease did King Azariah (Uzziah) have for the rest of his life?
kids.explanation: The LORD smote the king, so that he was a leper unto the day of his death, and dwelt in a several house (2 Kings 15:5).

kids.question: What did Shallum do to become king of Israel?
kids.option_a: He killed King Zechariah in front of the people
kids.option_b: He was chosen by the army commanders
kids.option_c: He was appointed by the high priest
kids.option_d: He was the son of the previous king
kids.explanation: Shallum the son of Jabesh conspired against him, and smote him before the people, and slew him, and reigned in his stead (2 Kings 15:10).

kids.question: How long did Shallum reign as king of Israel?
kids.explanation: Shallum the son of Jabesh reigned a full month in Samaria (2 Kings 15:13).

teens.question: What happened to Zechariah the son of Jeroboam II, and what prophecy did his death fulfil?
teens.option_a: He was slain by Shallum, fulfilling that Jehu's sons would sit on Israel's throne unto the fourth generation
teens.option_b: He was deposed and exiled to Assyria
teens.option_c: He died of plague after a six-month reign
teens.option_d: He was captured by the Syrians
teens.explanation: Shallum slew Zechariah, as the LORD had said unto Jehu, Thy sons shall sit on the throne of Israel unto the fourth generation (2 Kings 15:10, 12).

teens.question: What terrible act did Menahem commit against Tiphsah?
teens.option_a: He smote it and ripped up all the women therein that were with child because they opened not to him
teens.option_b: He burned it with fire and killed all the men
teens.option_c: He enslaved its population and sent them to Samaria
teens.option_d: He destroyed its temple and looted its treasury
teens.explanation: Menahem smote Tiphsah...and all the coasts thereof from Tirzah...because they opened not to him, therefore he smote it; and all the women therein that were with child he ripped up (2 Kings 15:16).

teens.question: How did Menahem raise the tribute money he paid to Tiglath-pileser of Assyria?
teens.option_a: He exacted it of Israel, even of all the mighty men of wealth, fifty shekels of silver of every man
teens.option_b: He emptied the temple treasury
teens.option_c: He sold northern territories to Assyria
teens.option_d: He taxed the merchants at the city gates
teens.explanation: Menahem exacted the money of Israel, even of all the mighty men of wealth, of each man fifty shekels of silver, to give to the king of Assyria (2 Kings 15:20).

teens.question: Which king of Israel was killed by Pekah the son of Remaliah?
teens.option_a: Pekahiah the son of Menahem
teens.option_b: Shallum the son of Jabesh
teens.option_c: Menahem himself
teens.option_d: Zechariah the son of Jeroboam
teens.explanation: Pekah the son of Remaliah, a captain of his, conspired against him, and smote him in Samaria...and killed him, and reigned in his room (2 Kings 15:25).

teens.question: Who killed Pekah and became the last king of Israel before its fall?
teens.option_a: Hoshea the son of Elah
teens.option_b: Menahem the son of Gadi
teens.option_c: Tiglath-pileser the Assyrian
teens.option_d: An unnamed captain from Gilead
teens.explanation: Hoshea the son of Elah made a conspiracy against Pekah the son of Remaliah, and smote him, and slew him, and reigned in his stead (2 Kings 15:30).

adults.question: Because Azariah was a leper, who judged the people of the land and governed the palace?
adults.option_a: Jotham the king's son, who was over the house, judging the people of the land
adults.option_b: The high priest as regent
adults.option_c: A council of seventy elders
adults.option_d: Azariah's wife as queen regent
adults.explanation: Jotham the king's son was over the house, judging the people of the land (2 Kings 15:5).

adults.question: What amount did Menahem pay Tiglath-pileser and what was the Assyrian king's purpose in accepting it?
adults.option_a: A thousand talents of silver, that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand
adults.option_b: Two thousand talents of gold to purchase peace for ten years
adults.option_c: Fifty shekels per city plus all captured prisoners
adults.option_d: The entire annual tribute of the temple treasury
adults.explanation: Menahem gave Pul a thousand talents of silver, that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand (2 Kings 15:19).

adults.question: How many men of wealth contributed to Menahem's tribute payment, given that each paid fifty shekels?
adults.option_a: Sixty thousand men, since a thousand talents equals 60,000 times 50 shekels
adults.option_b: Six hundred thousand, corresponding to the men of the Exodus
adults.option_c: Ten thousand, matching the fighting force of Israel
adults.option_d: One thousand, being the mighty men of the guard
adults.explanation: A thousand talents of silver at fifty shekels per man requires approximately 60,000 men of wealth, as 1 talent equals 3,000 shekels (2 Kings 15:19-20).

adults.question: Where was Pekahiah killed and who assisted Pekah in the assassination?
adults.option_a: In Samaria in the palace of the king's house, with Argob and Arieh, and fifty men of the Gileadites
adults.option_b: In the field at Jezreel with his personal guard
adults.option_c: At the gate of Samaria by the city elders
adults.option_d: At Bethel near the golden calf shrine
adults.explanation: Pekah smote him in Samaria, in the palace of the king's house...with Argob and Arieh, and with him fifty men of the Gileadites (2 Kings 15:25).

adults.question: What territories did Tiglath-pileser take from Israel during Pekah's reign?
adults.option_a: Ijon, Abel-beth-maachah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, Galilee, all the land of Naphtali and carried them captive to Assyria
adults.option_b: Only the Transjordan territories east of the Jordan
adults.option_c: The coastal plain from Megiddo to Dor
adults.option_d: Samaria and the hill country of Ephraim
adults.explanation: The king of Assyria came, and took Ijon, and Abel-beth-maachah, and Janoah, and Kedesh, and Hazor, and Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, and carried them captive to Assyria (2 Kings 15:29).

adults.question: How long did Azariah reign in Jerusalem and what is notable about his age at accession?
adults.option_a: Fifty and two years; he began to reign at sixteen years old
adults.option_b: Forty years; he began to reign at twenty
adults.option_c: Thirty years; he began to reign at twelve
adults.option_d: Twenty-five years; he began to reign at twenty-five
adults.explanation: Azariah was sixteen years old when he began to reign, and he reigned two and fifty years in Jerusalem (2 Kings 15:2).

adults.question: What pattern of political instability does chapter 15 reveal about the northern kingdom compared to Judah?
adults.option_a: Israel had five kings in about thirty years, most dying violently, while Judah had relative stability under Azariah's long reign
adults.option_b: Israel was more stable because its kings came from multiple strong dynasties
adults.option_c: Both kingdoms had equal political instability in this period
adults.option_d: Judah was more chaotic because Azariah's leprosy left the kingdom without leadership
adults.explanation: In the period of Azariah's 52-year reign in Judah, Israel cycled through Zechariah, Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, and Pekah — most killed violently — demonstrating the instability prophesied for a kingdom without a stable covenant dynasty (2 Kings 15:8-31).

adults.question: What does Tiglath-pileser III's appearance in this chapter signal for the trajectory of the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: It marks the beginning of direct Assyrian interference that would culminate in the complete destruction of Samaria in chapter 17
adults.option_b: It shows that Assyria was merely a neighbouring threat that Israel could manage by paying tribute
adults.option_c: It fulfils Isaiah's specific prophecy that Assyria would be Israel's permanent ally
adults.option_d: It is the first time a foreign king appears in Kings and sets a precedent of peaceful coexistence
adults.explanation: Tiglath-pileser III is paid tribute by Menahem and later depopulates Galilee and Transjordan (2 Kings 15:19, 29), initiating the Assyrian stranglehold on Israel that Sargon II would complete with the fall of Samaria in 722 BC (2 Kings 17).

kids.question: Who was the king of Judah in this chapter who did many wicked things?
kids.explanation: Ahaz the son of Jotham king of Judah began to reign (2 Kings 16:1).

kids.question: What did Ahaz do with his own son that was very wicked?
kids.option_a: He made his son pass through the fire
kids.option_b: He sold his son to Egypt
kids.option_c: He offered his son to a false prophet
kids.option_d: He sent his son away to Assyria
kids.explanation: He made his son to pass through the fire, according to the abominations of the heathen (2 Kings 16:3).

kids.question: Ahaz sent silver and gold from the temple to which powerful king to ask for help?
kids.explanation: Ahaz took the silver and gold that was found in the house of the LORD...and sent it for a present to the king of Assyria (2 Kings 16:8).

teens.question: What two kings attacked Ahaz of Judah in the Syro-Ephraimite crisis?
teens.option_a: Rezin king of Syria and Pekah the son of Remaliah king of Israel
teens.option_b: Hazael of Syria and Menahem of Israel
teens.option_c: Tiglath-pileser and Shalmaneser of Assyria
teens.option_d: The king of Egypt and the king of Edom
teens.explanation: Rezin king of Syria and Pekah son of Remaliah king of Israel came up to Jerusalem to war (2 Kings 16:5).

teens.question: What city did Rezin king of Syria recover for Syria during this crisis?
teens.option_a: Elath
teens.option_b: Kadesh
teens.option_c: Ramoth-gilead
teens.option_d: Hazor
teens.explanation: At that time Rezin king of Syria recovered Elath to Syria, and drave the Jews from Elath (2 Kings 16:6).

teens.question: What humiliating title did Ahaz use to describe himself when appealing to Tiglath-pileser for help?
teens.option_a: I am thy servant and thy son
teens.option_b: I am thy vassal and thine ally
teens.option_c: I am the least of thy servants
teens.option_d: I am a friend to thy house
teens.explanation: Ahaz sent messengers to Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria, saying, I am thy servant and thy son: come up, and save me (2 Kings 16:7).

teens.question: Where did Ahaz see an altar that he ordered Urijah the priest to copy for Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: In Damascus
teens.option_b: In Babylon
teens.option_c: In Nineveh
teens.option_d: In Samaria
teens.explanation: King Ahaz went to Damascus...and king Ahaz sent to Urijah the priest the fashion of the altar, and the pattern of it, according to all the workmanship thereof (2 Kings 16:10).

teens.question: What did Ahaz do to the bronze altar of the LORD after he installed the new Damascus altar?
teens.option_a: He moved it to the north side and used it only for his own enquiring; the king's burnt offering was on the new altar
teens.option_b: He melted it down and sent the bronze to Assyria
teens.option_c: He had it destroyed entirely
teens.option_d: He gave it to the priests for personal sacrifices
teens.explanation: The brazen altar shall be for me to enquire by. And Urijah the priest did according to all that king Ahaz commanded (2 Kings 16:15).

adults.question: How is Ahaz's wickedness compared to that of other kings in verse 3?
adults.option_a: He walked in the way of the kings of Israel, and made his son to pass through the fire, after the abominations of the heathen
adults.option_b: He was more wicked than Manasseh but less wicked than Ahab
adults.option_c: He did evil like Jeroboam the son of Nebat
adults.option_d: He sinned in the same manner as Baasha king of Israel
adults.explanation: He walked in the way of the kings of Israel, yea, and made his son to pass through the fire, according to the abominations of the heathen (2 Kings 16:3).

adults.question: What did Tiglath-pileser do in response to Ahaz's request and tribute?
adults.option_a: He hearkened unto him and went up against Damascus and took it and carried the people captive to Kir and slew Rezin
adults.option_b: He accepted the tribute but did not attack Syria
adults.option_c: He attacked Israel instead of Syria
adults.option_d: He promised help but only sent a token force
adults.explanation: The king of Assyria hearkened unto him: for the king of Assyria went up against Damascus, and took it, and carried the people of it captive to Kir, and slew Rezin (2 Kings 16:9).

adults.question: What changes did Ahaz make to the temple furnishings upon returning from Damascus?
adults.option_a: He cut off the borders of the bases and removed the laver, took down the sea from the bronze oxen and put it on a stone pavement, and removed the Sabbath canopy
adults.option_b: He removed only the golden lampstands and sent them to Assyria
adults.option_c: He destroyed the inner sanctuary and built a new one
adults.option_d: He relocated the ark of the covenant to the palace treasury
adults.explanation: Ahaz cut off the borders of the bases, and removed the laver from off them...and he removed the brasen sea from off the brasen oxen, and put it upon a pavement of stones (2 Kings 16:17).

adults.question: What was Ahaz's stated reason for moving the bronze sea and altering the temple entry?
adults.option_a: Because of the king of Assyria
adults.option_b: Because the priests requested it
adults.option_c: Because the temple floor was cracked
adults.option_d: Because it was needed for a new religious ceremony
adults.explanation: The covert for the sabbath that they had built in the house, and the king's entry without, turned he from the house of the LORD for the king of Assyria (2 Kings 16:18).

adults.question: What does Ahaz's appeal to Tiglath-pileser as "thy servant and thy son" represent in diplomatic terms?
adults.option_a: A formal declaration of vassal submission, placing Judah under Assyrian suzerainty
adults.option_b: A polite diplomatic formula with no binding obligations
adults.option_c: An adoption ceremony making Ahaz a royal heir of Assyria
adults.option_d: A military alliance between equals
adults.explanation: I am thy servant and thy son (2 Kings 16:7) was standard ancient Near Eastern vassal language, meaning Ahaz formally submitted Judah as a client state of Assyria — a betrayal Isaiah vigorously condemned (cf. Isaiah 7).

adults.question: What happened to the Edomites in Elath after Rezin recovered the port city?
adults.option_a: The Syrians came to Elath and dwelt there unto this day
adults.option_b: They were expelled and the Israelites returned
adults.option_c: They allied with Judah against Assyria
adults.option_d: They were enslaved by the Assyrians
adults.explanation: He drave the Jews from Elath: and the Syrians came to Elath, and dwelt there unto this day (2 Kings 16:6).

adults.question: What does Urijah the priest's compliance with Ahaz's altar orders reveal about the priesthood?
adults.option_a: The priesthood had become subservient to royal authority, compromising its prophetic role of correcting wayward kings
adults.option_b: The priests were legally bound to obey the king in all matters of temple organisation
adults.option_c: Urijah was a false priest who had always been corrupt
adults.option_d: The Mosaic law permitted the king to redesign temple furnishings
adults.explanation: Urijah the priest built an altar according to all that king Ahaz had sent from Damascus...thus did Urijah the priest (2 Kings 16:11), showing a priesthood that failed to stand against royal idolatry as the law required.

adults.question: How does Isaiah 7 provide essential background for understanding Ahaz's choices in this chapter?
adults.option_a: Isaiah offered Ahaz a sign from God and urged him to trust the LORD rather than Assyria during the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, but Ahaz refused — making his appeal to Tiglath-pileser an act of deliberate unbelief
adults.option_b: Isaiah approved of Ahaz's alliance as a necessary political decision
adults.option_c: Isaiah predicted that Syria and Israel would destroy Judah if Ahaz did not seek Assyrian help
adults.option_d: Isaiah was silent during Ahaz's reign and only prophesied under Hezekiah
adults.explanation: God through Isaiah told Ahaz "Ask thee a sign of the LORD" (Isaiah 7:11) but Ahaz refused, choosing Tiglath-pileser instead — a decision 2 Kings 16 records as the fulfilment of Ahaz's faithless policy that ultimately brought Assyria permanently into Judah's affairs.

kids.question: Who was the last king of Israel before it was conquered?
kids.explanation: In the twelfth year of Ahaz king of Judah began Hoshea the son of Elah to reign in Samaria over Israel (2 Kings 17:1).

kids.question: Which great empire took the people of Israel away captive?
kids.explanation: The king of Assyria came up throughout all the land, and went up to Samaria, and besieged it three years...and the king of Assyria did carry away Israel unto Assyria (2 Kings 17:5-6).

kids.question: What animals did God send to attack the foreign settlers who did not worship Him?
kids.explanation: The LORD sent lions among them, which slew some of them (2 Kings 17:25).

teens.question: How long did Shalmaneser besiege Samaria before it fell?
teens.option_a: Three years
teens.option_b: Two years
teens.option_c: Seven years
teens.option_d: One year
teens.explanation: The king of Assyria besieged it three years (2 Kings 17:5).

teens.question: What was the main sin that caused Israel to be exiled, according to the chapter's explanation?
teens.option_a: They feared other gods and walked in the statutes of the heathen whom the LORD cast out
teens.option_b: They refused to pay tribute to Assyria
teens.option_c: They stopped making sacrifices in the temple
teens.option_d: They expelled the prophets of the LORD
teens.explanation: The children of Israel...feared other gods, and walked in the statutes of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out from before the children of Israel (2 Kings 17:7-8).

teens.question: To what place did the Assyrians carry away the Israelites?
teens.option_a: Halah and Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes
teens.option_b: Nineveh and the plains of Assyria
teens.option_c: Babylon and the land of Shinar
teens.option_d: Egypt and the desert of Libya
teens.explanation: The king of Assyria carried Israel away into Assyria, and put them in Halah and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes (2 Kings 17:6).

teens.question: Why did the king of Assyria send back a captured Israelite priest to Bethel?
teens.option_a: To teach the new settlers the manner of the God of the land, because lions were killing them
teens.option_b: To translate the law of Moses into their language
teens.option_c: To maintain peace between the settlers and the Assyrian governor
teens.option_d: To serve as a political prisoner
teens.explanation: The king of Assyria commanded, saying, Carry thither one of the priests whom ye brought from thence; and let them go and dwell there, and let him teach them the manner of the God of the land (2 Kings 17:27-28).

teens.question: What was the mixed religious practice of the new settlers called in this chapter?
teens.option_a: They feared the LORD and served their own gods — they were not true worshippers
teens.option_b: Syncretism approved by the Assyrian king
teens.option_c: The way of Samaria accepted by the LORD
teens.option_d: Partial obedience which pleased God
teens.explanation: They feared the LORD, and served their own gods, after the manner of the nations whom they carried away from thence (2 Kings 17:33).

adults.question: What act of Hoshea triggered the Assyrian invasion that ended the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: He sent messengers to So king of Egypt and brought no present to the king of Assyria as he had done year by year
adults.option_b: He stopped worshipping the golden calves at Bethel and Dan
adults.option_c: He made an alliance with Damascus against Assyria
adults.option_d: He invaded Judah seeking to unite the kingdoms
adults.explanation: Hoshea sent messengers to So king of Egypt, and brought no present to the king of Assyria, as he had done year by year: therefore the king of Assyria shut him up, and bound him in prison (2 Kings 17:4).

adults.question: What does the phrase "the LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight" echo from earlier in Kings?
adults.option_a: The repeated prophetic warnings from Moses and the prophets that covenant unfaithfulness would result in exile from the land
adults.option_b: The curse of Balaam on Israel in the wilderness
adults.option_c: The prayer of Solomon at the temple dedication that God would forgive if they sinned
adults.option_d: The threat made by Elijah to Ahab after the vineyard of Naboth
adults.explanation: The LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight (2 Kings 17:18), fulfilling the curses of Deuteronomy 28-29 and the repeated warnings of prophets sent to Israel (2 Kings 17:13).

adults.question: What people groups were settled in the cities of Samaria to replace the exiled Israelites?
adults.option_a: From Babylon, and from Cuthah, and from Ava, and from Hamath, and from Sepharvaim
adults.option_b: From Nineveh and the cities of Persia
adults.option_c: From Moab, Edom, and the Philistine plain
adults.option_d: From Egypt and the western coast of the Mediterranean
adults.explanation: The king of Assyria brought men from Babylon, and from Cuthah, and from Ava, and from Hamath, and from Sepharvaim, and placed them in the cities of Samaria (2 Kings 17:24).

adults.question: What gods did the men of Babylon and Cuth bring with them to Samaria?
adults.option_a: Succoth-benoth; the men of Cuth made Nergal
adults.option_b: Baal and Asherah from their homeland
adults.option_c: Chemosh and Milcom from Moab and Ammon
adults.option_d: Dagon and Ashtaroth from the coastal Philistines
adults.explanation: The men of Babylon made Succoth-benoth, and the men of Cuth made Nergal, and the men of Hamath made Ashima (2 Kings 17:30).

adults.question: What specific sin does the narrator emphasise that Jeroboam drove Israel into, and why was it so significant?
adults.option_a: He made Israel walk in all his sins, driving them away from following the LORD with two golden calves at Bethel and Dan — a cultic perversion that persisted until the exile
adults.option_b: He introduced human sacrifice which was the final cause of exile
adults.option_c: He removed the ark of the covenant from Jerusalem
adults.option_d: He made Israel abandon the Sabbath entirely
adults.explanation: They followed the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat...and the children of Israel did secretly those things that were not right against the LORD...even from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city (2 Kings 17:22, 9).

adults.question: How many gods did the new settler population of Samaria ultimately worship alongside the LORD?
adults.option_a: They feared the LORD yet served their own gods — a dual worship the text condemns as not true fear of the LORD
adults.option_b: They abandoned all foreign gods after the lions attacked them
adults.option_c: They served only the LORD after the priest taught them
adults.option_d: They worshipped three gods in rotation
adults.explanation: They fear not the LORD, neither do they after their statutes, or after their ordinances, or after the law and commandment which the LORD commanded the children of Jacob (2 Kings 17:34).

adults.question: What does the phrase "unto this day" (used repeatedly in chapter 17) tell us about the author's perspective?
adults.option_a: The narrator was writing after the exile and could observe that the syncretistic practices of the Samaritan settlers were still ongoing
adults.option_b: The narrator was an eyewitness of the Assyrian conquest writing immediately after
adults.option_c: The phrase indicates the events were very recent
adults.option_d: The phrase is a scribal addition from a later editor with no theological significance
adults.explanation: So do they unto this day (2 Kings 17:41) shows the narrator writing from a post-exilic perspective, confirming the ongoing Samaritan syncretism as still present in his day.

adults.question: What theological purpose does the extended commentary in verses 7-23 serve in the book of Kings as a whole?
adults.option_a: It provides a definitive theological verdict on the northern kingdom's history, explaining that exile was the just result of centuries of covenant faithlessness despite divine patience and prophetic warning
adults.option_b: It is an editorial aside with no bearing on the narrative of Judah
adults.option_c: It simply records historical facts without theological interpretation
adults.option_d: It primarily blames the Assyrian empire rather than Israel's sin
adults.explanation: The commentary of 2 Kings 17:7-23 is the theological climax of the northern kingdom narrative, demonstrating that Samaria's fall was not a military accident but the fulfilment of covenantal curses (Deuteronomy 28) long threatened through every prophet God sent to Israel (2 Kings 17:13).

kids.question: What king of Judah trusted in the LORD and removed the high places and broke down the idols?
kids.explanation: Hezekiah...removed the high places, and brake the images, and cut down the groves, and brake in pieces the brasen serpent that Moses had made (2 Kings 18:3-4).

kids.question: What did Hezekiah break into pieces because the people were burning incense to it?
kids.option_a: The brasen serpent that Moses had made
kids.option_c: An Asherah pole in the temple
kids.option_d: The altar at the high place
kids.explanation: He brake in pieces the brasen serpent that Moses had made: for unto those days the children of Israel did burn incense to it (2 Kings 18:4).

kids.question: What was the name of the Assyrian officer who came and spoke against Hezekiah and the LORD?
kids.explanation: The king of Assyria sent Rabshakeh from Lachish to Jerusalem unto king Hezekiah with a great host (2 Kings 18:17).

teens.question: What is the spiritual summary given of Hezekiah's character?
teens.option_a: He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him
teens.option_b: He was as righteous as David but fell short of Solomon in wisdom
teens.option_c: He did right but did not remove the high places
teens.option_d: He followed the LORD only in the early years of his reign
teens.explanation: He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him (2 Kings 18:5).

teens.question: What were the two things Hezekiah refused to do in relation to the king of Assyria?
teens.option_a: He rebelled against the king of Assyria and served him not
teens.option_b: He paid tribute and refused military alliance
teens.option_c: He hid in the temple and refused to negotiate
teens.option_d: He surrendered the temple treasury but refused to open the city gates
teens.explanation: He rebelled against the king of Assyria, and served him not (2 Kings 18:7).

teens.question: How much tribute did Hezekiah initially give Sennacherib after his invasion of Judah?
teens.option_a: Three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold
teens.option_b: A thousand talents of silver and a hundred talents of gold
teens.option_c: Fifty talents each of silver and gold
teens.option_d: Two hundred talents of silver and fifty of gold
teens.explanation: Hezekiah gave him three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold (2 Kings 18:14).

teens.question: What claim did Rabshakeh make about the LORD's role in the Assyrian invasion?
teens.option_a: The LORD said to me, Go up against this land, and destroy it
teens.option_b: The LORD has abandoned Hezekiah because he destroyed the high places
teens.option_c: The gods of all nations are powerless against Assyria
teens.option_d: The LORD cannot hear prayers from within Jerusalem's walls
teens.explanation: Rabshakeh said, Am I now come up without the LORD against this place to destroy it? The LORD said to me, Go up against this land, and destroy it (2 Kings 18:25).

teens.question: Why did Hezekiah's officials ask Rabshakeh to speak in the Syrian language rather than the Jews' language?
teens.option_a: So that the people on the wall would not hear and be frightened
teens.option_b: So that spies from Assyria could not understand the reply
teens.option_c: Because Hebrew was considered a sacred language not to be used in negotiations
teens.option_d: So that the negotiations could be kept secret from the priests
