teens.explanation: Speak, I pray thee, to thy servants in the Syrian language...and talk not with us in the Jews' language in the ears of the people that are on the wall (2 Kings 18:26).

adults.question: What Mosaic artefact did Hezekiah destroy and what name did he give it?
adults.option_a: The bronze serpent that Moses had made, calling it Nehushtan
adults.option_b: The tent of meeting that had been in Shiloh
adults.option_c: The golden ephod of Gideon that Israel had made an idol
adults.option_d: The stone tablets of the law that were cracked
adults.explanation: He brake in pieces the brasen serpent that Moses had made: for unto those days the children of Israel did burn incense to it: and he called it Nehushtan (2 Kings 18:4).

adults.question: How did Hezekiah strip the temple to pay Sennacherib's tribute demand?
adults.option_a: He cut off the gold from the doors of the temple of the LORD, and from the pillars which Hezekiah king of Judah had overlaid, and gave it to the king of Assyria
adults.option_b: He melted the golden lampstands and the altar of incense
adults.option_c: He sold the priestly vestments and the silver basins
adults.option_d: He removed the ark of the covenant and gave it as a pledge
adults.explanation: Hezekiah cut off the gold from the doors of the temple of the LORD, and from the pillars which Hezekiah king of Judah had overlaid, and gave it to the king of Assyria (2 Kings 18:16).

adults.question: What four arguments did Rabshakeh make against Judah's ability to withstand Assyria?
adults.option_a: Egypt is a broken reed, Hezekiah destroyed the LORD's own altars, Judah lacks horses, and the LORD commanded Assyria to come
adults.option_b: Judah's army is weak, its walls are old, its allies have all fallen, and its food supply is cut off
adults.option_c: Hezekiah is a false king, the priests are corrupt, the people are unfaithful, and God is angry with Jerusalem
adults.option_d: Assyria has never been defeated, its gods are the strongest, its army is the largest, and surrender is the only reasonable choice
adults.explanation: Rabshakeh argued: Egypt is a broken reed (v.21); Hezekiah has removed the LORD's high places (v.22); Judah cannot supply horsemen for even two thousand horses (v.23); the LORD commanded this invasion (v.25) (2 Kings 18:21-25).

adults.question: What did Rabshakeh promise the people of Jerusalem if they surrendered?
adults.option_a: A land like your own land, a land of corn and wine, a land of bread and vineyards, a land of oil olive and of honey, that ye may live, and not die
adults.option_b: Safe passage to the cities of Judah unharmed
adults.option_c: Freedom from taxation for thirty years
adults.option_d: They could keep their property and return within two generations
adults.explanation: Hearken not to Hezekiah: for thus saith the king of Assyria, Make an agreement with me by a present, and come out to me, and then eat ye every man of his own vine...until I come and take you away to a land like your own land (2 Kings 18:31-32).

adults.question: How did the people of Jerusalem respond to Rabshakeh's speech?
adults.option_a: They answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not
adults.option_b: They shouted defiance from the walls
adults.option_c: They wept and begged Hezekiah to surrender
adults.option_d: They threw stones at the Assyrian delegation
adults.explanation: The people held their peace, and answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not (2 Kings 18:36).

adults.question: What is the significance of Rabshakeh's comparison of the LORD to the gods of other nations?
adults.option_a: It constitutes a direct blasphemy, setting up the theological confrontation resolved in chapter 19 where the LORD demonstrates his uniqueness over all gods
adults.option_b: It was simply a diplomatic rhetorical strategy with no religious significance
adults.option_c: Rabshakeh was himself a believer who misquoted the LORD
adults.option_d: It proved that Assyria had already defeated the god of Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Rabshakeh said, Hath any of the gods of the nations delivered at all his land out of the hand of the king of Assyria? (2 Kings 18:33) — this challenge to the LORD's uniqueness sets up Hezekiah's prayer and the divine response in chapter 19.

adults.question: How did Hezekiah's officials react when they returned to him after hearing Rabshakeh?
adults.option_a: They came to Hezekiah with their clothes rent and told him the words of Rabshakeh
adults.option_b: They resigned their positions out of fear
adults.option_c: They convened the elders to plan a surrender
adults.option_d: They sent a secret messenger to Egypt
adults.explanation: They came to Hezekiah with their clothes rent, and told him the words of Rabshakeh (2 Kings 18:37).

adults.question: What theological irony is present in Rabshakeh's claim that the LORD commanded Assyria to attack, in light of the book of Isaiah?
adults.option_a: The claim contains a grain of truth — God did use Assyria as an instrument of judgment — but Rabshakeh twisted it into a counsel of despair, while Isaiah affirmed God would also judge Assyria for its arrogance
adults.option_b: Rabshakeh's claim was entirely false and the LORD never used Assyria for any divine purpose
adults.option_c: The claim correctly summarises Isaiah's entire prophecy
adults.option_d: Rabshakeh was a secret Hebrew prophet preaching surrender as the will of God
adults.explanation: Isaiah 10:5-6 confirms that Assyria was indeed the rod of God's anger against sinful Israel, but Assyria exceeded its mandate through pride — so while Rabshakeh's claim had theological basis (cf. 2 Kings 18:25), Isaiah 10:12-16 records God's judgment on Assyria for its arrogance.

kids.question: What did Hezekiah do when he heard the threatening words of Assyria?
kids.option_a: He went into the house of the LORD
kids.option_d: He wrote a letter to Egypt
kids.explanation: When king Hezekiah heard it, that he rent his clothes, and covered himself with sackcloth, and went into the house of the LORD (2 Kings 19:1).

kids.question: Which prophet did Hezekiah send to for help against the Assyrians?
kids.explanation: He sent Eliakim...unto Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz (2 Kings 19:2).

kids.question: How many Assyrian soldiers did the angel of the LORD kill in one night?
kids.explanation: The angel of the LORD went out, and smote in the camp of the Assyrians a hundred fourscore and five thousand (2 Kings 19:35).

teens.question: What did Isaiah tell Hezekiah's messengers about Sennacherib's fate?
teens.option_a: He shall hear a rumour, and shall return to his own land; and I will cause him to fall by the sword in his own land
teens.option_b: He shall be struck blind and wander in the wilderness
teens.option_c: His army shall be destroyed by plague and he shall flee to Nineveh
teens.option_d: He shall be defeated in battle outside Jerusalem's walls
teens.explanation: Thus saith the LORD, Be not afraid of the words which thou hast heard...Behold, I will send a blast upon him, and he shall hear a rumour, and shall return to his own land; and I will cause him to fall by the sword in his own land (2 Kings 19:6-7).

teens.question: What key theological claim did Hezekiah make in his prayer about the gods of the other nations?
teens.option_a: They are no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone
teens.option_b: They are demons who deceive the nations
teens.option_c: They are fallen angels who rule the nations
teens.option_d: They have some power but the LORD is greater
teens.explanation: Of a truth, LORD, the kings of Assyria have destroyed the nations and their lands, and have cast their gods into the fire: for they were no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone (2 Kings 19:17-18).

teens.question: What was the sign Isaiah gave regarding the survival of Judah?
teens.option_a: Ye shall eat this year such things as grow of themselves, and in the second year that which springeth of the same; and in the third year sow ye
teens.option_b: A star would appear over Jerusalem for three nights
teens.option_c: The walls of the city would shine with light for forty days
teens.option_d: Rain would fall in the dry season as proof of deliverance
teens.explanation: And this shall be a sign unto thee, Ye shall eat this year such things as grow of themselves, and in the second year that which springeth of the same; and in the third year sow ye, and reap (2 Kings 19:29).

teens.question: How did Sennacherib die, and who killed him?
teens.option_a: He was slain by his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer with the sword while worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god
teens.option_b: He died of a plague that swept through Nineveh
teens.option_c: He was executed by Esarhaddon for losing his army
teens.option_d: He died peacefully of old age in Nineveh
teens.explanation: It came to pass, as he was worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god, that Adrammelech and Sharezer his sons smote him with the sword (2 Kings 19:37).

teens.question: What image did Isaiah use to describe Sennacherib's arrogance before God?
teens.option_a: He had boasted with the height of the mountains and thought himself the axe that could boast against him that heweth
teens.option_b: He compared himself to the sun rising over all the earth
teens.option_c: He claimed to be greater than the gods of heaven
teens.option_d: He said his arm was stronger than the arm of the LORD
teens.explanation: By the multitude of my chariots I am come up to the height of the mountains...I have digged and drunk strange waters (2 Kings 19:23-24), showing Sennacherib's boast that his own power accomplished everything.

adults.question: How did Hezekiah physically handle Sennacherib's threatening letter?
adults.option_a: He went up into the house of the LORD, and spread it before the LORD
adults.option_b: He tore it in pieces and returned it to the messengers
adults.option_c: He gave it to Isaiah for interpretation
adults.option_d: He posted it on the city gates as a warning to the people
adults.explanation: Hezekiah received the letter of the hand of the messengers, and read it: and Hezekiah went up into the house of the LORD, and spread it before the LORD (2 Kings 19:14).

adults.question: What theological basis did Hezekiah use as the foundation of his prayer for deliverance?
adults.option_a: That the LORD alone is God of all the kingdoms of the earth, thou hast made heaven and earth
adults.option_b: That Israel had kept the covenant faithfully for three generations
adults.option_c: That Solomon's temple was the most holy place on earth
adults.option_d: That the Davidic promise guaranteed Jerusalem's protection
adults.explanation: Hezekiah prayed, O LORD God of Israel, which dwellest between the cherubims, thou art the God, even thou alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth; thou hast made heaven and earth (2 Kings 19:15).

adults.question: What does Isaiah's oracle say the LORD had purposed long before Sennacherib's campaigns?
adults.option_a: Hast thou not heard long ago how I have done it, and of ancient times that I have formed it? now have I brought it to pass
adults.option_b: The LORD had not planned Assyria's campaigns but was permitting them
adults.option_c: Sennacherib's victories were entirely due to his own military genius
adults.option_d: God had warned Sennacherib not to attack but he refused
adults.explanation: Hast thou not heard long ago how I have done it, and of ancient times that I have formed it? now have I brought it to pass (2 Kings 19:25), showing that Assyria's campaigns were within God's sovereign plan.

adults.question: What specific insult to God does Isaiah's oracle identify as provoking divine action against Sennacherib?
adults.option_a: By thy messengers thou hast reproached the LORD, and hast said, With the multitude of my chariots I am come up to the height of the mountains
adults.option_b: He dared to approach the gates of Jerusalem which was God's holy city
adults.option_c: He demanded tribute from the temple treasury
adults.option_d: He claimed to be a god equal to the LORD
adults.explanation: By thy messengers thou hast reproached the LORD, and hast said, With the multitude of my chariots I am come up to the height of the mountains, to the sides of Lebanon (2 Kings 19:23).

adults.question: What divine promise does Isaiah make about the remnant of the house of Judah?
adults.option_a: The remnant that is escaped of the house of Judah shall yet again take root downward, and bear fruit upward
adults.option_b: Jerusalem shall be utterly destroyed but rebuilt in forty years
adults.option_c: The remnant shall go into exile but return in seventy years
adults.option_d: The house of David shall rule forever without interruption
adults.explanation: The remnant that is escaped of the house of Judah shall yet again take root downward, and bear fruit upward (2 Kings 19:30).

adults.question: What four-part promise did the LORD make about what Sennacherib would NOT do to Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: He shall not come into this city, nor shoot an arrow there, nor come before it with shield, nor cast a bank against it
adults.option_b: He shall not enter the temple, touch the ark, harm the priests, or defile the altar
adults.option_c: He shall not kill the king, enslave the people, burn the temple, or break the walls
adults.option_d: He shall not destroy the city, remove the people, take the treasure, or cut down the trees
adults.explanation: He shall not come into this city, nor shoot an arrow there, nor come before it with shield, nor cast a bank against it (2 Kings 19:32).

adults.question: What does the phrase "for my own sake, and for my servant David's sake" reveal about God's motivation for defending Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: God's defence of Jerusalem rested simultaneously on His own honour and on the Davidic covenant — not merely on Hezekiah's righteousness
adults.option_b: God defended Jerusalem only because Hezekiah was personally righteous
adults.option_c: The Davidic covenant had no bearing and God acted purely to preserve His temple
adults.option_d: God's sole motivation was to demonstrate His power to Assyria and Egypt
adults.explanation: I will defend this city, to save it, for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake (2 Kings 19:34), showing that the defence rested on God's own name and His covenant with David (2 Samuel 7).

adults.question: What does the destruction of 185,000 Assyrians by a single angel demonstrate theologically about divine warfare?
adults.option_a: God requires no human army to accomplish His purposes; one heavenly agent is sufficient against the world's greatest military power
adults.option_b: It proves that Jerusalem had a larger garrison than Assyria realised
adults.option_c: It shows that plagues are God's primary instrument against wicked nations
adults.option_d: It demonstrates the superiority of Israelite military strategy under Hezekiah
adults.explanation: The angel of the LORD smote in the camp of the Assyrians a hundred fourscore and five thousand (2 Kings 19:35), showing that the LORD of hosts fights for His people without requiring human military strength, echoing the divine warrior theme throughout Scripture.

kids.question: What happened to Hezekiah that made him very sick and almost die?
kids.option_b: He was bitten by a serpent
kids.option_c: He fell from the temple wall
kids.explanation: In those days was Hezekiah sick unto death (2 Kings 20:1).

kids.question: How many extra years did God promise to add to Hezekiah's life after he prayed?
kids.explanation: I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears: behold, I will heal thee: on the third day thou shalt go up unto the house of the LORD. And I will add unto thy days fifteen years (2 Kings 20:5-6).

kids.question: What sign did God perform with the sundial to confirm His promise to Hezekiah?
kids.option_a: He made the shadow go backward ten degrees
kids.option_b: He made the sun shine through the night
kids.option_c: He made the shadow stand still for one hour
kids.option_d: He turned the shadow to gold
kids.explanation: He brought the shadow ten degrees backward, by which it had gone down in the dial of Ahaz (2 Kings 20:11).

teens.question: What was Hezekiah told by Isaiah before God extended his life?
teens.option_a: Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live
teens.option_b: Repent of thy sins or die before the year is out
teens.option_c: Surrender to Assyria or be taken into captivity
teens.option_d: Make a sacrifice at the temple or the LORD will not hear thy prayer
teens.explanation: Isaiah said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live (2 Kings 20:1).

teens.question: What did Hezekiah do after receiving Isaiah's death sentence?
teens.option_a: He turned his face to the wall, and prayed unto the LORD, and wept sore
teens.option_b: He called a fast for all Jerusalem
teens.option_c: He offered a great sacrifice at the temple
teens.option_d: He summoned all the elders to pray with him
teens.explanation: Then he turned his face to the wall, and prayed unto the LORD, saying...And Hezekiah wept sore (2 Kings 20:2-3).

teens.question: What was the poultice prescribed to heal Hezekiah's boil?
teens.option_a: A lump of figs laid upon the boil
teens.option_b: A mixture of oil and wine applied with a cloth
teens.option_c: Cedar bark soaked in water
teens.option_d: Ground hyssop mixed with honey
teens.explanation: Isaiah said, Take a lump of figs. And they took and laid it on the boil, and he recovered (2 Kings 20:7).

teens.question: From which country did messengers come bearing letters and a present for Hezekiah?
teens.option_a: Babylon, sent by Berodach-baladan the son of Baladan
teens.option_b: Egypt, sent by the Pharaoh
teens.option_c: Persia, sent by Cyrus
teens.option_d: Syria, sent by the new king of Damascus
teens.explanation: Berodach-baladan, the son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent letters and a present unto Hezekiah: for he had heard that Hezekiah had been sick (2 Kings 20:12).

teens.question: What warning did Isaiah give Hezekiah after he showed the Babylonian envoys all his treasures?
teens.option_a: Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house...shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left
teens.option_b: The Babylonians will return in ten years to destroy Jerusalem
teens.option_c: God will strike Hezekiah with leprosy for showing his wealth to foreigners
teens.option_d: The Babylonian king will make war on Judah within one generation
teens.explanation: Isaiah said, Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house, and that which thy fathers have laid up in store unto this day, shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left, saith the LORD (2 Kings 20:17).

adults.question: On what basis did Hezekiah appeal to God in his prayer for healing?
adults.option_a: I beseech thee, O LORD, remember now how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart, and have done that which is good in thy sight
adults.option_b: On the basis of the Davidic covenant and his descent from the king after God's own heart
adults.option_c: He promised to give the entire temple treasury as an offering if healed
adults.option_d: He reminded God of his having destroyed the brazen serpent and removed the high places
adults.explanation: Hezekiah prayed, I beseech thee, O LORD, remember now how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart, and have done that which is good in thy sight. And Hezekiah wept sore (2 Kings 20:3).

adults.question: How quickly did God reverse His word to Hezekiah through Isaiah?
adults.option_a: Before Isaiah had gone out into the middle court, the word of the LORD came to him again
adults.option_b: On the following morning after Hezekiah's prayer
adults.option_c: Three days later when Hezekiah entered the temple
adults.option_d: Seven days of prayer and fasting later
adults.explanation: It came to pass, afore Isaiah was gone out into the middle court, that the word of the LORD came to him, saying, Turn again (2 Kings 20:4).

adults.question: What title did God use for Himself in Isaiah's message that revealed the covenantal basis for hearing Hezekiah's prayer?
adults.option_a: The LORD, the God of David thy father
adults.option_b: The LORD of hosts, the Holy One of Israel
adults.option_c: The LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt
adults.option_d: The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
adults.explanation: Thus saith the LORD, the God of David thy father (2 Kings 20:5), grounding the healing promise in the Davidic covenant.

adults.question: Which of the two sign options did Hezekiah choose and why did he make that choice?
adults.option_a: He chose the shadow going backward rather than forward, saying it is a light thing for the shadow to go down ten degrees
adults.option_b: He chose the shadow going forward as a more natural sign
adults.option_c: He left the choice to Isaiah the prophet
adults.option_d: He refused a sign saying he trusted God without confirmation
adults.explanation: Hezekiah answered, It is a light thing for the shadow to go down ten degrees: nay, but let the shadow return backward ten degrees. And Isaiah the prophet cried unto the LORD: and he brought the shadow ten degrees backward (2 Kings 20:10-11).

adults.question: What did Hezekiah show the Babylonian envoys that provoked Isaiah's condemnation?
adults.option_a: All the house of his precious things, the silver, and the gold, and the spices, and the precious ointment, and all the house of his armour, and all that was found in his treasures: there was nothing in his house, nor in all his dominion, that Hezekiah shewed them not
adults.option_b: Only the temple treasury and the new bronze altar
adults.option_c: The plans for Jerusalem's fortifications and water supply
adults.option_d: The ark of the covenant and the priestly garments
adults.explanation: Hezekiah shewed them all the house of his precious things, the silver, and the gold, and the spices, and the precious ointment, and all the house of his armour, and all that was found in his treasures: there was nothing in his house, nor in all his dominion, that Hezekiah shewed them not (2 Kings 20:13).

adults.question: What would happen to Hezekiah's sons according to Isaiah's prophecy?
adults.option_a: They shall be taken away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon
adults.option_b: They shall rule Israel and Judah as co-regents
adults.option_c: They shall be killed when Babylon destroys Jerusalem
adults.option_d: They shall go into Egypt and serve the Pharaoh
adults.explanation: Of thy sons that shall issue from thee...shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon (2 Kings 20:18).

adults.question: How did Hezekiah respond to Isaiah's prophecy of Babylonian exile?
adults.option_a: Good is the word of the LORD...Is it not good, if peace and truth be in my days?
adults.option_b: He tore his robe and cried to God for mercy on his descendants
adults.option_c: He repented in sackcloth and the prophecy was cancelled
adults.option_d: He imprisoned Isaiah for speaking such words against the king's house
adults.explanation: Hezekiah said unto Isaiah, Good is the word of the LORD which thou hast spoken. And he said, Is it not good, if peace and truth be in my days? (2 Kings 20:19).

adults.question: What does Hezekiah's tunnel and pool, mentioned in verse 20, represent historically?
adults.option_a: An extraordinary feat of engineering — the Siloam Tunnel — confirmed by archaeology and a discovered inscription, dug to secure Jerusalem's water supply during the Assyrian siege
adults.option_b: A symbolic water offering to the LORD made during Hezekiah's sickness
adults.option_c: The irrigation system built during the fifteen added years
adults.option_d: A defensive moat dug around Jerusalem's walls
adults.explanation: He made a pool, and a conduit, and brought water into the city (2 Kings 20:20). The Siloam Tunnel (Hezekiah's Tunnel) was discovered in the 19th century, and the Siloam Inscription found within it confirms the biblical account of its construction.

kids.question: Who was the wickedest king of Judah, who reigned for fifty-five years?
kids.explanation: Manasseh was twelve years old when he began to reign, and reigned five and fifty years in Jerusalem (2 Kings 21:1).

kids.question: What terrible thing did Manasseh do to his own son?
kids.option_a: He made his son pass through the fire
kids.option_b: He sold his son to a foreign king
kids.option_c: He banished his son from the palace
kids.option_d: He offered his son to a false prophet
kids.explanation: He made his son pass through the fire, and observed times, and used enchantments, and dealt with familiar spirits and wizards (2 Kings 21:6).

kids.question: How long did Amon, Manasseh's son, reign before he was killed?
kids.explanation: Amon was twenty and two years old when he began to reign, and he reigned two years in Jerusalem (2 Kings 21:19).

teens.question: What abomination did Manasseh set up inside the temple itself?
teens.option_a: A carved image of Asherah
teens.option_b: The golden calf of Jeroboam
teens.option_c: An altar to Baal covered with bronze
teens.option_d: A carved image of Nisroch the Assyrian god
teens.explanation: He set a graven image of the grove that he had made in the house, of which the LORD said to David, and to Solomon his son, In this house, and in Jerusalem...will I put my name for ever (2 Kings 21:7).

teens.question: What verdict did God pronounce on Jerusalem because of Manasseh's sins?
teens.option_a: I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down
teens.option_b: Jerusalem shall burn like Sodom and Gomorrah
teens.option_c: Jerusalem shall be scattered to the four winds
teens.option_d: Jerusalem shall become a desolation like the wilderness
teens.explanation: I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down (2 Kings 21:13).

teens.question: With what did God say He would measure and plumb Jerusalem for judgment?
teens.option_a: The line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab
teens.option_b: The measuring rod of Moses and the plumb line of the prophets
teens.option_c: The standard of the nations and the cord of Egypt
teens.option_d: The rod of the Assyrian king and the cord of Babylon
teens.explanation: I will stretch over Jerusalem the line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab (2 Kings 21:13).

teens.question: Who killed King Amon, and who then became king?
teens.option_a: His own servants; then Josiah his son
teens.option_b: A conspiracy of princes; then Jehoahaz
teens.option_c: An unnamed prophet; then Hezekiah's grandson
teens.option_d: The people of the land; then his brother
teens.explanation: His servants conspired against him, and slew the king in his own house. And the people of the land slew all them that had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead (2 Kings 21:23-24).

teens.question: According to the prophets, what specific sin filled Jerusalem from one end to another?
teens.option_a: Innocent blood
teens.option_b: Idolatry and sorcery
teens.option_c: Theft and false witness
teens.option_d: Adultery and violence
teens.explanation: Manasseh shed innocent blood very much, till he had filled Jerusalem from one end to another (2 Kings 21:16).

adults.question: What specific comparison is made between Manasseh's sins and the nations God expelled from Canaan?
adults.option_a: He did after the abominations of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out before the children of Israel
adults.option_b: His sins exceeded those of the Canaanites in every respect
adults.option_c: He restored only the practices that the Canaanites had abandoned
adults.option_d: His sins were similar to but not as severe as those of the Amorites
adults.explanation: He did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, after the abominations of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out before the children of Israel (2 Kings 21:2).

adults.question: What altars did Manasseh build in the two courts of the house of the LORD?
adults.option_a: He built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD
adults.option_b: He built altars to Baal and Asherah in the inner and outer courts
adults.option_c: He built a single great altar to Molech in the outer court
adults.option_d: He built altars to the gods of Assyria in the treasury courts
adults.explanation: He built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD (2 Kings 21:5).

adults.question: How does the text describe Manasseh's seduction of Judah into sin?
adults.option_a: Manasseh seduced them to do more evil than did the nations whom the LORD destroyed before the children of Israel
adults.option_b: He forced them through legal decrees to worship the Asherah
adults.option_c: He deceived the priests into accepting foreign worship as Mosaic
adults.option_d: He bribed the prophets to endorse his religious innovations
adults.explanation: Manasseh seduced them to do more evil than did the nations whom the LORD destroyed before the children of Israel (2 Kings 21:9).

adults.question: What does the oracle in verses 12-15 say will happen to everyone who hears of Jerusalem's coming judgment?
adults.option_a: Both his ears shall tingle
adults.option_b: The nations shall tremble at the sound of her fall
adults.option_c: All who hear shall clap their hands and rejoice
adults.option_d: The prophets of the nations shall foretell it
adults.explanation: Whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle (2 Kings 21:12).

adults.question: What does the phrase "the LORD's anger was kindled against Judah" following Manasseh's reign signal about the subsequent narrative of Kings?
adults.option_a: It establishes that even Josiah's later reforms could not reverse the divine decision to exile Judah, as confirmed in 2 Kings 23:26
adults.option_b: It means God immediately sent Babylon to destroy Jerusalem in Manasseh's lifetime
adults.option_c: It is merely a formulaic expression with no bearing on later events
adults.option_d: It was reversed when Manasseh repented late in his reign
adults.explanation: Notwithstanding the LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath (2 Kings 23:26), and 2 Kings 21:15 states that Manasseh's sins provoked God's anger — confirmed by Jeremiah 15:4 which names Manasseh as the cause of Judah's eventual exile.

adults.question: What occult practices are explicitly listed as part of Manasseh's sin?
adults.option_a: He observed times, used enchantments, dealt with familiar spirits and wizards
adults.option_b: He offered burnt sacrifices to stars and cast lots before the Asherah
adults.option_c: He employed Philistine diviners and consulted the Urim illegally
adults.option_d: He sacrificed white bulls at each new moon to Baal
adults.explanation: He observed times, and used enchantments, and dealt with familiar spirits and wizards: he wrought much wickedness in the sight of the LORD (2 Kings 21:6).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the fact that the people of the land executed Amon's assassins and made Josiah king?
adults.option_a: It shows a popular loyalty to the Davidic line that resisted palace coups, preserving the covenant dynasty
adults.option_b: It shows the army controlled the succession to the throne
adults.option_c: It indicates the people were entirely wicked and chose Josiah for political reasons
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a specific prophecy made to Solomon at the temple dedication
adults.explanation: The people of the land slew all them that had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead (2 Kings 21:24), showing a popular instinct to maintain the Davidic line even in a morally degraded age.

adults.question: How does 2 Chronicles 33 supplement the account in 2 Kings 21 regarding Manasseh's ultimate fate?
adults.option_a: 2 Chronicles records that Manasseh was taken captive to Babylon, humbled himself, prayed to God, was restored to Jerusalem, and removed foreign altars — a repentance not mentioned in Kings
adults.option_b: 2 Chronicles confirms every detail of 2 Kings 21 without addition
adults.option_c: 2 Chronicles records that Manasseh died in Assyrian captivity and was never restored
adults.option_d: 2 Chronicles says Manasseh built the temple portico as an act of contrition
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:11-13 records that the Assyrians took Manasseh captive, and when he was in affliction, he besought the LORD...and God was intreated of him — a repentance the Kings account omits, perhaps because Kings judges him by the lasting effect of his sins on Judah.

kids.question: What important book was found in the temple during Josiah's repairs?
kids.option_b: The book of the kings of Judah
kids.option_d: The book of Deuteronomy of Ezra
kids.explanation: Hilkiah the high priest said unto Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD (2 Kings 22:8).

kids.question: How old was Josiah when he became king?
kids.explanation: Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned thirty and one years in Jerusalem (2 Kings 22:1).

kids.question: Who was the prophetess that Josiah's servants went to consult about the book of the law?
kids.explanation: Hilkiah the priest...went unto Huldah the prophetess (2 Kings 22:14).

teens.question: What was Josiah's overall spiritual assessment compared to other kings?
teens.option_a: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in all the way of David his father, and turned not aside to the right hand or to the left
teens.option_b: He was righteous but did not remove the high places
teens.option_c: He was as good as Hezekiah but less faithful than David
teens.option_d: He followed the LORD only until the book was found
teens.explanation: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in all the way of David his father, and turned not aside to the right hand or to the left (2 Kings 22:2).

teens.question: How did Josiah react when the book of the law was read to him?
teens.option_a: He rent his clothes
teens.option_b: He called an immediate fast
teens.option_c: He wept before the LORD for seven days
teens.option_d: He summoned all the priests to read it again
teens.explanation: When the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes (2 Kings 22:11).

teens.question: Who was Hilkiah the high priest and what else is he notable for in this chapter?
teens.option_a: He found the book of the law and sent it to Shaphan the scribe, who read it before the king
teens.option_b: He repaired the temple treasury and reorganised the Levitical service
teens.option_c: He was the father of Jeremiah the prophet
teens.option_d: He wrote the book of the law and then hid it for safekeeping
teens.explanation: Hilkiah the high priest said unto Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD. And Hilkiah gave the book to Shaphan, and he read it (2 Kings 22:8).

teens.question: Where did Huldah the prophetess live in Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: In the college
teens.option_b: In the palace district
teens.option_c: In the temple precincts
teens.option_d: In the lower city
teens.explanation: She dwelt in Jerusalem in the college (2 Kings 22:14).

teens.question: What did Huldah prophesy would happen to Josiah personally because he humbled himself?
teens.option_a: He would be gathered to his grave in peace, and his eyes would not see all the evil coming on Jerusalem
teens.option_b: He would live to see Babylon's defeat and Jerusalem's restoration
teens.option_c: He would be given thirty more years of life like Hezekiah
teens.option_d: He would be honoured above all kings of Judah before him
teens.explanation: Because thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself before the LORD...thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace; and thine eyes shall not see all the evil which I will bring upon this place (2 Kings 22:19-20).

adults.question: What specific work was being done on the temple when the book of the law was found?
adults.option_a: The house was being repaired with money collected at the door by the Levites, disbursed to carpenters, builders, and masons
adults.option_b: A new outer court was being constructed on the north side
adults.option_c: The bronze pillars were being replaced with new ones
adults.option_d: The inner sanctuary was being re-gilded with donated gold
adults.explanation: Shaphan...told the king, saying, Thy servants have gathered the money that was found in the house, and have delivered it into the hand of them that do the work, that have the oversight of the house of the LORD (2 Kings 22:9).

adults.question: To whom did King Josiah instruct Hilkiah to go and enquire of the LORD?
adults.option_a: Go ye, enquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah, concerning the words of this book that is found
adults.option_b: Go to the elders of Judah and ask them to interpret the law
adults.option_c: Go to the temple in Bethel where the Levites keep records
adults.option_d: Go to the Urim and Thummim for a divine answer
adults.explanation: The king commanded Hilkiah the priest...saying, Go ye, enquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah, concerning the words of this book that is found (2 Kings 22:12-13).

adults.question: What specific message did Huldah declare about the fate of Jerusalem and Judah?
adults.option_a: Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read
adults.option_b: Jerusalem shall be spared for forty years if the people repent
adults.option_c: The evil shall come but not in the generation of the king's sons
adults.option_d: A remnant shall be preserved in the city even when Babylon comes
adults.explanation: Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read (2 Kings 22:16).

adults.question: What was the sin that Huldah identified as the reason God's wrath was not quenched?
adults.option_a: They have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands
adults.option_b: They had shed innocent blood in the temple courts
adults.option_c: They had stopped keeping the Passover for three generations
adults.option_d: They had disobeyed the law on tithes and firstfruits
adults.explanation: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched (2 Kings 22:17).

adults.question: What is the scholarly consensus about the identity of the "book of the law" found in the temple?
adults.option_a: Most scholars identify it as Deuteronomy or a substantial portion thereof, based on the reforms Josiah subsequently undertook which parallel Deuteronomic law
adults.option_b: It was the complete Pentateuch transcribed by Ezra from memory
adults.option_c: It was the book of the covenant from Exodus 20-23 only
adults.option_d: It was a prophetic collection mistakenly called the law
adults.explanation: The book found in the temple (2 Kings 22:8) is widely identified with Deuteronomy because Josiah's subsequent reforms — centralising worship, abolishing high places, celebrating Passover — all correspond to the specific requirements of Deuteronomy.

adults.question: What does the tenderness of Josiah's heart, cited by Huldah, contrast with in the broader narrative of Kings?
adults.option_a: It contrasts sharply with Manasseh's hardened heart that filled Jerusalem with innocent blood and Amon's brief, unreformed reign
adults.option_b: It contrasts with Hezekiah's weeping, showing Josiah responded more actively
adults.option_c: It contrasts with Solomon's wisdom, showing that humility surpasses knowledge
adults.option_d: It contrasts with the prophets who warned Israel without personal grief
adults.explanation: Huldah said, because thine heart was tender (2 Kings 22:19), in sharp contrast to Manasseh who shed innocent blood very much (2 Kings 21:16) and Amon who did evil and humbled not himself (2 Chronicles 33:23).

adults.question: What does Huldah's role as prophetess suggest about the status of women in prophetic ministry in Israel?
adults.option_a: Women could hold genuine prophetic authority; Josiah's court treated her word as authoritative on par with male prophets including Jeremiah and Zephaniah who were contemporary
adults.option_b: She was consulted only because the male prophets were unavailable
adults.option_c: She was a lesser prophetess whose word required male confirmation
adults.option_d: Her role was exceptional and had no precedent or successor in Israel
adults.explanation: Josiah's delegation went to Huldah the prophetess (2 Kings 22:14), treating her oracle as authoritative — notably at a time when both Jeremiah and Zephaniah were active prophets, suggesting she was the court's preferred prophetic voice.

adults.question: How does the promise that Josiah would be gathered to his grave in peace relate to his actual death at Megiddo in 2 Kings 23:29?
adults.option_a: The peace refers to his not living to witness the Babylonian sack of Jerusalem rather than dying peacefully in battle — he was spared the greater calamity, not guaranteed a natural death
adults.option_b: The prophecy was simply wrong because Josiah disobeyed God at Megiddo
adults.option_c: Josiah died peacefully of wounds sustained at Megiddo in his own palace
adults.option_d: The peace referred to his eternal rest with his ancestors in the covenantal promise
adults.explanation: Thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace (2 Kings 22:20) means Josiah would not see the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem — his death at Megiddo (2 Kings 23:29) preceded the exile, so he was spared witnessing Jerusalem's fall, which is the specific evil Huldah described.

kids.question: What great feast did Josiah restore that had not been kept properly for a very long time?
kids.explanation: The king commanded all the people, saying, Keep the passover unto the LORD your God (2 Kings 23:21).

kids.question: Where was King Josiah killed in battle?
kids.explanation: Pharaoh-nechoh...slew him at Megiddo, when he had seen him (2 Kings 23:29).

kids.question: What did Josiah do to the altars and high places throughout the land?
kids.option_a: He defiled and broke them down
kids.option_b: He offered sacrifices on them to the LORD
kids.option_d: He left them standing but unused
kids.explanation: He defiled Topheth...and he brake down the altars that were on the top of the upper chamber of Ahaz...and brake down the high places (2 Kings 23:10, 12).

teens.question: What was the covenant Josiah made standing by a pillar in the temple?
teens.option_a: To walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all their heart and all their soul
teens.option_b: To rebuild the temple entirely with pure cedar from Lebanon
teens.option_c: To execute all priests who had served at the high places
teens.option_d: To make annual war against all idolatrous nations surrounding Judah
teens.explanation: The king stood by a pillar, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all their heart and all their soul (2 Kings 23:3).

teens.question: What ancient prophecy from 1 Kings 13 did Josiah fulfil at Bethel?
teens.option_a: He burned men's bones upon the altar at Bethel, defiling the high place of Jeroboam, just as a man of God had cried against the altar by the word of the LORD
teens.option_b: He tore down the golden calf of Bethel as a prophet had foretold
teens.option_c: He built a new altar at Bethel to replace Jeroboam's, as prophesied
teens.option_d: He killed the priests of Bethel as an unnamed prophet had foretold three hundred years earlier
teens.explanation: He burnt men's bones upon it, and defiled it, according to the word of the LORD which the man of God proclaimed, who proclaimed these words (2 Kings 23:16).

teens.question: What statement is made about Josiah compared to all other kings of Judah?
teens.option_a: Like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might
teens.option_b: He was greater than David in obedience and greater than Solomon in wisdom
teens.option_c: He was the most militarily successful king Judah had ever produced
teens.option_d: He was the only king to fulfil every word of the prophets
teens.explanation: Like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might, according to all the law of Moses; neither after him arose there any like him (2 Kings 23:25).

teens.question: What was the result of Josiah's unparalleled righteousness on God's plan to judge Judah?
teens.option_a: The LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath because of all the provocations of Manasseh
teens.option_b: God cancelled the judgment entirely as He had done for Hezekiah
teens.option_c: God delayed the exile for one hundred years
teens.option_d: God sent only a partial judgment destroying the north but sparing Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Notwithstanding the LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath, wherewith his anger was kindled against Judah, because of all the provocations that Manasseh had provoked him withal (2 Kings 23:26).

teens.question: Who succeeded Josiah and what happened to him?
teens.option_a: Jehoahaz his son reigned three months then Pharaoh-nechoh put him in bands at Riblah and made Eliakim king in his place
teens.option_b: Jehoiachin his son reigned three months then was taken to Babylon
teens.option_c: Zedekiah his son reigned eleven years then fled from Jerusalem
teens.option_d: Jehoram his son reigned two years then was removed by the people of the land
teens.explanation: Pharaoh-nechoh put him in bands at Riblah in the land of Hamath, that he might not reign in Jerusalem (2 Kings 23:33).

adults.question: What happened to the book of the covenant in the context of the renewal ceremony described in verse 2?
adults.option_a: The king read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant which was found in the house of the LORD
adults.option_b: It was placed in the ark of the covenant for safekeeping
adults.option_c: It was copied by the priests and distributed to every city
adults.option_d: It was translated into the language of the northern exiles
adults.explanation: The king went up into the house of the LORD...and he read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant which was found in the house of the LORD (2 Kings 23:2).

adults.question: What was the cultic site of Topheth and what did Josiah do to it?
adults.option_a: It was a site in the valley of Hinnom where children were burned in sacrifice to Molech; Josiah defiled it so it could no longer be used
adults.option_b: It was the sacred grove of Asherah in the Kidron Valley; Josiah cut it down
adults.option_c: It was a high place north of Jerusalem; Josiah demolished it and burned the stones
adults.option_d: It was the pagan altar built by Manasseh in the temple courts; Josiah smashed it
adults.explanation: He defiled Topheth, which is in the valley of the children of Hinnom, that no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech (2 Kings 23:10).

adults.question: What did Josiah do with the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun?
adults.option_a: He took away the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun at the entering in of the house of the LORD, and burned the chariots of the sun with fire
adults.option_b: He slaughtered them as a sacrifice at the temple
adults.option_c: He gave them to the Levites for the temple service
adults.option_d: He sent them to Egypt as tribute
adults.explanation: He took away the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun, at the entering in of the house of the LORD...and burned the chariots of the sun with fire (2 Kings 23:11).

adults.question: What was extraordinary about the Passover Josiah kept, compared to the entire history of the judges and kings?
adults.option_a: Surely there was not holden such a passover from the days of the judges that judged Israel, nor in all the days of the kings of Israel, nor of the kings of Judah
adults.option_b: It was celebrated with a larger number of animals than any previous Passover
adults.option_c: It was the first Passover ever celebrated outside Jerusalem
adults.option_d: It lasted fourteen days instead of the customary seven
adults.explanation: Surely there was not holden such a passover from the days of the judges that judged Israel, nor in all the days of the kings of Israel, nor of the kings of Judah (2 Kings 23:22).

adults.question: What did Josiah do to the priests of the high places, distinguishing between those in Judah and those in Samaria?
adults.option_a: He slew all the priests of the high places...in Samaria; but in Judah he brought the priests of the high places to Jerusalem but did not allow them to minister at the altar
adults.option_b: He executed all priests of high places in both Judah and Samaria equally
adults.option_c: He restored the priests of Judah's high places to temple service after they repented
adults.option_d: He sent the priests of Samaria to Assyria and retired the priests of Judah to their towns
adults.explanation: He slew all the priests of the high places...in Samaria (2 Kings 23:20); but in Judah he brought all the priests of the high places to Jerusalem...but they did not come up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem (2 Kings 23:8-9).

adults.question: What was the name Pharaoh-nechoh gave to the new king he installed after deposing Jehoahaz, and what does the name change signify?
adults.option_a: He made Eliakim the son of Josiah king in the room of Josiah his father, and turned his name to Jehoiakim — a name change signifying Egyptian suzerainty over Judah
adults.option_b: He installed Zedekiah and changed his name to mark Babylonian submission
adults.option_c: He renamed Jehoahaz as Shallum to mark his captivity
adults.option_d: He named a new king from outside the Davidic line as a vassal ruler
adults.explanation: Pharaoh-nechoh made Eliakim the son of Josiah king in the room of Josiah his father, and turned his name to Jehoiakim (2 Kings 23:34). Name changes in the ancient Near East signalled sovereign authority over the renamed person.

adults.question: What taxation did Pharaoh-nechoh impose on Judah and how did Jehoiakim collect it?
adults.option_a: He taxed the land a hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold; Jehoiakim exacted the silver and the gold of the people of the land, of every one according to his taxation
adults.option_b: He demanded annual tribute of fifty talents of silver paid from the temple treasury
adults.option_c: He required Judah to provide one thousand soldiers for Egyptian campaigns
adults.option_d: He imposed a per-capita head tax of one shekel on every male over twenty
adults.explanation: He taxed the land a hundred talents of silver, and a talent of gold. And Jehoiakim gave the silver and the gold to Pharaoh; but he taxed the land to give the money according to the commandment of Pharaoh: he exacted the silver and the gold of the people of the land, of every one according to his taxation (2 Kings 23:33, 35).

adults.question: What theological tension does Josiah's death at Megiddo create in the context of Huldah's prophecy and the Deuteronomic principle that obedience brings blessing?
adults.option_a: It challenges simplistic retribution theology — the most righteous king died young in battle, forcing readers to trust in God's larger redemptive purposes and eschatological justice rather than immediate earthly reward
adults.option_b: It proves that Deuteronomic theology was wrong and righteousness does not affect outcomes
adults.option_c: It shows that Josiah must have sinned secretly and his death was punishment
adults.option_d: It confirms that the covenant blessings applied only to the nation as a whole, never to individual kings
adults.explanation: Josiah — the most faithful king in Israel's history (2 Kings 23:25) — died at Megiddo at the hand of Pharaoh-nechoh (2 Kings 23:29), a fact that challenged Israel's understanding of divine justice and pointed forward to the suffering servant who would also die obedient and apparently abandoned (cf. Isaiah 53).

kids.question: Which great king came against Jerusalem and made Jehoiakim his servant for three years?
kids.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came up, and Jehoiakim became his servant three years (2 Kings 24:1).

kids.question: Who was taken captive to Babylon with all his household, servants, princes, and officers?
kids.explanation: Jehoiachin the king of Judah went out to the king of Babylon, he, and his mother, and his servants, and his princes, and his officers (2 Kings 24:12).

kids.question: Who did Nebuchadnezzar make king of Judah after taking Jehoiachin captive?
kids.option_a: Mattaniah, whose name he changed to Zedekiah
kids.explanation: The king of Babylon made Mattaniah his father's brother king in his stead, and changed his name to Zedekiah (2 Kings 24:17).

teens.question: What enemies did God send against Jehoiakim because of the sins of Manasseh?
teens.option_a: Bands of the Chaldees, bands of the Syrians, bands of the Moabites, and bands of the children of Ammon
teens.option_b: The full army of Babylon alone
teens.option_c: The Philistines from the west and the Edomites from the south
teens.option_d: Pestilence, famine, and wild animals as covenant curses
teens.explanation: The LORD sent against him bands of the Chaldees, and bands of the Syrians, and bands of the Moabites, and bands of the children of Ammon (2 Kings 24:2).

teens.question: How long did Jehoiachin reign before he surrendered to Nebuchadnezzar?
teens.option_a: Three months
teens.option_b: One year
teens.option_c: Six months
teens.option_d: Three years
teens.explanation: Jehoiachin was eighteen years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem three months (2 Kings 24:8).

teens.question: What treasures did Nebuchadnezzar carry out of the temple and palace?
teens.option_a: He carried out thence all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon had made
teens.option_b: Only the silver and gold, leaving the sacred vessels untouched
teens.option_c: The ark of the covenant and the bronze altar
teens.option_d: All the grain stores and livestock of Jerusalem
teens.explanation: He carried out thence all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the LORD (2 Kings 24:13).

teens.question: How many people were carried away in the deportation of Jehoiachin?
teens.option_a: Ten thousand captives, including all the princes, mighty men, craftsmen, and smiths; only the poorest people remained
teens.option_b: Twenty thousand captives from across all Judah
teens.option_c: Five thousand captives, only from Jerusalem
teens.option_d: One thousand of the nobility and priests
teens.explanation: He carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths (2 Kings 24:14).

teens.question: What was Zedekiah's relationship to Jehoiachin, and how long did he reign?
teens.option_a: Zedekiah was Jehoiachin's uncle; he reigned eleven years
teens.option_b: Zedekiah was Jehoiachin's brother; he reigned seven years
teens.option_c: Zedekiah was Jehoiachin's son; he reigned three months
teens.option_d: Zedekiah was Jehoiachin's cousin; he reigned fifteen years
teens.explanation: Zedekiah was twenty and one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem...he was the uncle of Jehoiachin, i.e. the king of Babylon made his father's brother king (2 Kings 24:17-18).

adults.question: What theological explanation is given for the disasters coming upon Judah in Jehoiakim's reign?
adults.option_a: Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh
adults.option_b: It was simply the consequence of political miscalculation in rebelling against Babylon
adults.option_c: God was testing Judah's faith as He had tested Abraham
adults.option_d: The judgment came because Jehoiakim had broken the specific covenant Josiah had made
adults.explanation: Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh, according to all that he did (2 Kings 24:3).

adults.question: What specific wording underlines that the disaster on Judah was not merely human political agency?
adults.option_a: The LORD would not pardon
adults.option_b: Nebuchadnezzar acted independently of any divine purpose
adults.option_c: The prophets had warned but God had changed His mind
adults.option_d: The people repented but it was too late for this generation
adults.explanation: For through the anger of the LORD it came to pass in Jerusalem and Judah, until he had cast them out from his presence (2 Kings 24:20), making clear that Babylon was God's instrument, not merely a geopolitical power.

adults.question: What does the removal of all the craftsmen and smiths from Jerusalem signify strategically and symbolically?
adults.option_a: Strategically it crippled Judah's ability to manufacture weapons; symbolically it stripped the city of the skilled labour needed to maintain its structures and self-sufficiency
adults.option_b: It was purely punitive with no military motive
adults.option_c: It fulfilled a specific prophecy of Amos about Jerusalem's artisans
adults.option_d: It was done to rebuild Babylon's temples using Judahite skilled workers
adults.explanation: All the craftsmen and smiths...none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land (2 Kings 24:14), leaving Jerusalem economically and militarily defenceless, unable to forge weapons or maintain walls — a deliberate Babylonian strategy of subjugation.

adults.question: How does the deportation of Jehoiachin relate to Jeremiah's prophetic ministry?
adults.option_a: Jeremiah 22:24-30 explicitly addressed Jehoiachin (Coniah), declaring him a "despised broken idol" and pronouncing that no descendant would sit on David's throne — making the continuation of the Messianic line through his line theologically complex
adults.option_b: Jeremiah approved of Jehoiachin and urged surrender as God's will
adults.option_c: Jeremiah's prophecy about Jehoiachin was the one prediction that did not come true
adults.option_d: Jeremiah never mentioned Jehoiachin by name
adults.explanation: Jeremiah 22:28-30 asked, Is this man Coniah a despised broken idol?...thus saith the LORD, Write ye this man childless...for no man of his seed shall prosper, sitting upon the throne of David — a prophecy Matthew 1 resolves by tracing Jesus's legal Davidic line through Jehoiachin but biological line through Nathan (Luke 3).

adults.question: What name change does the text record for Zedekiah and what does it imply about his status?
adults.option_a: His name was Mattaniah before Nebuchadnezzar renamed him Zedekiah, marking him as a Babylonian vassal king ruling at Babylon's pleasure
adults.option_b: His name was Shallum and the people renamed him Zedekiah at his coronation
adults.option_c: He renamed himself Zedekiah as a religious act of dedicating his reign to the LORD
adults.option_d: Nebuchadnezzar renamed him from Eliakim to Zedekiah
adults.explanation: The king of Babylon made Mattaniah...king in his stead, and changed his name to Zedekiah (2 Kings 24:17), a public name-change signalling Babylonian suzerainty, paralleling the Egyptian renaming of Eliakim to Jehoiakim (2 Kings 23:34).

adults.question: What spiritual assessment is given of Zedekiah?
adults.option_a: He did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim had done
adults.option_b: He was righteous in his early years but fell into idolatry when Jerusalem was besieged
adults.option_c: He was the most wicked king since Manasseh
adults.option_d: He did what was right in the sight of the LORD like his ancestor Hezekiah
adults.explanation: He did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim had done (2 Kings 24:19).

adults.question: What event triggers the final chapter's narrative of Jerusalem's destruction?
adults.option_a: Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon
adults.option_b: A Babylonian invasion launched without provocation
adults.option_c: Zedekiah sought help from Egypt and Babylon discovered the plot
adults.option_d: An earthquake destroyed Jerusalem's walls making them indefensible
adults.explanation: Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon (2 Kings 24:20), setting in motion the siege and destruction narrated in chapter 25.

adults.question: How does the removal of the temple vessels and cutting of Solomon's golden vessels in chapter 24 foreshadow the complete destruction of chapter 25?
adults.option_a: It represents the first phase of the temple's despoliation — the stripping of its wealth — before the second and final phase of its physical destruction, showing a progressive intensification of divine judgment
adults.option_b: It fulfilled a specific prophecy of Isaiah that the vessels would be taken before the building was burned
adults.option_c: It was an act of mercy allowing the sacred objects to survive even if the building was destroyed
adults.option_d: It had no symbolic significance and was purely motivated by Babylon's greed
adults.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar cut in pieces all the vessels of gold (2 Kings 24:13) in this first deportation; chapter 25 records the temple itself being burned and the remaining bronze pillars carried to Babylon (2 Kings 25:13-17), completing the progressive dismantling of Solomon's temple.

kids.question: What did the Babylonians do to the temple in Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: They burned the house of the LORD with fire
kids.option_b: They carried it stone by stone to Babylon
kids.option_c: They turned it into a palace for the governor
kids.option_d: They filled it with Babylonian idols
kids.explanation: He burnt the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem, and every great man's house burnt he with fire (2 Kings 25:9).

kids.question: What happened to King Zedekiah when the Babylonians caught him?
kids.option_a: They killed his sons before his eyes, then put out his eyes and bound him in chains
kids.option_b: He was taken to Babylon and made a servant of the king
kids.option_c: He was killed immediately at the walls of Jerusalem
kids.option_d: He was allowed to live in peace in Jerusalem
kids.explanation: They slew the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters of brass, and carried him to Babylon (2 Kings 25:7).

kids.question: Who was the kind foreign king who freed Jehoiachin from prison at the end of the book?
kids.explanation: Evil-merodach king of Babylon in the year that he began to reign did lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison (2 Kings 25:27).

teens.question: In what year of Zedekiah's reign did Nebuchadnezzar come with all his army and besiege Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: The ninth year, tenth month, tenth day
teens.option_b: The seventh year, fifth month, first day
teens.option_c: The eleventh year, fourth month, ninth day
teens.option_d: The fifth year, third month, seventeenth day
teens.explanation: In the ninth year of his reign, in the tenth month, in the tenth day of the month, that Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came, he, and all his host, against Jerusalem (2 Kings 25:1).

teens.question: Through which gate did Zedekiah flee Jerusalem, and where was he overtaken?
teens.option_a: The gate between two walls; the plains of Jericho
teens.option_b: The fish gate; the valley of the Jordan
teens.option_c: The sheep gate; the wilderness of Judah
teens.option_d: The water gate; the Dead Sea shore
teens.explanation: The king went the way toward the plain...And the army of the Chaldees pursued after the king, and overtook him in the plains of Jericho (2 Kings 25:4-5).

teens.question: Who was Nebuzar-adan and what was his role?
teens.option_a: The captain of the guard; he was the Babylonian officer who burned Jerusalem and oversaw the deportation
teens.option_b: The chief priest of Babylon who supervised the destruction of the temple
teens.option_c: A Babylonian general who commanded the army besieging Jerusalem
teens.option_d: The governor appointed over the remaining people of Judah
teens.explanation: Nebuzar-adan, captain of the guard, a servant of the king of Babylon, came unto Jerusalem: And he burnt the house of the LORD (2 Kings 25:8-9).

teens.question: Who was appointed governor over the people who remained in Judah?
teens.option_a: Gedaliah the son of Ahikam
teens.option_b: Nebuzar-adan the captain of the guard
teens.option_c: An unnamed Babylonian official
teens.option_d: Johanan the son of Careah
teens.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon made Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan, ruler over the people (2 Kings 25:22).

teens.question: What was the final act of hope at the end of 2 Kings?
teens.option_a: Evil-merodach lifted up Jehoiachin's head, released him from prison, spoke kindly to him, and gave him a seat above all the kings with him in Babylon
teens.option_b: A remnant returned from Babylon to Jerusalem to rebuild the altar
teens.option_c: A prophet declared that Israel would be restored within seventy years
teens.option_d: Gedaliah rebuilt the walls of Jerusalem with the remaining people
teens.explanation: Evil-merodach king of Babylon...did lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison...And changed his prison garments: and he did eat bread continually before him all the days of his life (2 Kings 25:27-29).

adults.question: How long was Jerusalem besieged and what finally broke the city's resistance?
adults.option_a: The siege lasted until the eleventh year of Zedekiah; the famine prevailed in the city and there was no bread for the people
adults.option_b: The siege lasted two years until Babylon destroyed the aqueducts
adults.option_c: The city fell in six months when its garrison mutinied
adults.option_d: Jerusalem held out for three years until Egypt failed to send relief
adults.explanation: The city was besieged unto the eleventh year of king Zedekiah. And on the ninth day of the fourth month the famine prevailed in the city, and there was no bread for the people of the land (2 Kings 25:2-3).

adults.question: What was the fate of Seraiah the chief priest and Zephaniah the second priest?
adults.option_a: Nebuzar-adan took them to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah, and Nebuchadnezzar put them to death
adults.option_b: They were taken captive to Babylon along with the nobles
adults.option_c: They escaped with Zedekiah toward the Jordan
adults.option_d: They were placed under house arrest in the ruined temple
adults.explanation: Nebuzar-adan the captain of the guard took Seraiah the chief priest, and Zephaniah the second priest...And he carried them to the king of Babylon to Riblah: and the king of Babylon smote them, and slew them at Riblah (2 Kings 25:18, 20-21).

adults.question: What happened to the bronze pillars, the sea, and the bases Solomon had made for the temple?
adults.option_a: The Chaldees brake them in pieces, and carried the brass of them to Babylon
adults.option_b: They were buried under the temple floor by the priests before the Babylonians arrived
adults.option_c: They were taken whole to Babylon and placed in Nebuchadnezzar's palace
adults.option_d: They were melted down in Jerusalem and the metal used to pay the army
adults.explanation: The pillars of brass that were in the house of the LORD, and the bases, and the brasen sea that was in the house of the LORD, did the Chaldees break in pieces, and carried the brass of them to Babylon (2 Kings 25:13).

adults.question: Who murdered Gedaliah the governor and what was the consequence for the remaining people of Judah?
adults.option_a: Ishmael the son of Nethaniah of the royal seed smote him; and all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the forces arose and came to Egypt for fear of the Chaldees
adults.option_b: Unnamed bandits killed Gedaliah and the people dispersed to surrounding nations
adults.option_c: The captain Johanan killed Gedaliah and established his own rule over Judah
adults.option_d: The Ammonites killed Gedaliah and Nebuchadnezzar appointed a new governor
adults.explanation: Ishmael the son of Nethaniah...came, and ten men with him, and smote Gedaliah...And all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the forces, arose, and came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees (2 Kings 25:25-26).

adults.question: How does the detail of the pillar measurements in verses 17-22 function literarily in the conclusion of Kings?
adults.option_a: It echoes the original construction account in 1 Kings 7, forming a literary inclusio that marks the complete reversal of the Solomonic era — what Solomon built, Babylon destroyed
adults.option_b: It serves as a historical inventory for the exilic community to catalogue their losses
adults.option_c: It proves that the author was an eyewitness to the temple's destruction
adults.option_d: It fulfils a specific law requiring all temple measurements to be recorded
adults.explanation: The detailed pillar measurements (2 Kings 25:17) recall 1 Kings 7:15-16 where Solomon first erected them, forming a deliberate literary bracket showing the full dissolution of Solomon's kingdom and temple — the theological nadir of the entire narrative of Kings.

adults.question: What is the significance of Nebuchadnezzar being at Riblah as the place where judgment on Judah's leaders was executed?
adults.option_a: Riblah was also where Pharaoh-nechoh had bound Jehoahaz after Josiah's death — making it the place where both Egyptian and Babylonian overlords executed their authority over Judah, symbolising complete subjugation
adults.option_b: Riblah was chosen because it was the geographic centre between Judah and Babylon
adults.option_c: Riblah was the sacred city of the Aramean gods and executions there were ritual acts
adults.option_d: Riblah had no particular significance and was simply a convenient military headquarters
adults.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar slew Judah's leaders at Riblah (2 Kings 25:20-21), the same place Pharaoh-nechoh had deposed Jehoahaz (2 Kings 23:33), showing that Judah had become utterly subject first to Egypt and then to Babylon — both empires exercising life-and-death power at the same northern Syrian location.

adults.question: What does the release of Jehoiachin in verses 27-30 contribute to the theological conclusion of Kings?
adults.option_a: It ends the book on a note of restrained hope — the Davidic line is not extinct but surviving, even if in exile, leaving open the possibility of future fulfilment of the covenant promises to David
adults.option_b: It proves that Babylon had repented of its destruction of Jerusalem
adults.option_c: It fulfils Jeremiah's prophecy that Jehoiachin would return to Jerusalem within his lifetime
adults.option_d: It shows that the exile was ending and the return under Zerubbabel was imminent
adults.explanation: Evil-merodach lifted up the head of Jehoiachin and set his throne above the kings with him in Babylon (2 Kings 25:28), ending the book not in total despair but with the Davidic heir surviving — a fragile but real sign that the covenant with David (2 Samuel 7) had not been finally revoked.

adults.question: How does the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC function as the theological climax not only of 2 Kings but of the entire Deuteronomistic History from Deuteronomy through Kings?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates that the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28-29 were fully enacted — the nation received every threatened judgment for persistent unfaithfulness, validating both God's justice and His word through Moses and the prophets
adults.option_b: It proves that God had permanently abandoned Israel with no future restoration
adults.option_c: It shows that the Mosaic covenant was inferior to the later Davidic covenant which ultimately prevailed
adults.option_d: It ends the historical narrative without any theological conclusion, leaving the reader to draw their own interpretation
adults.explanation: The fall of Jerusalem (2 Kings 25) fulfils Deuteronomy 28:36, 64 — the king taken captive, the people scattered among the nations — vindicating every covenant warning from Moses through the classical prophets, while the survival of the Davidic heir (2 Kings 25:27-30) sustains hope for the new covenant and Messianic fulfilment foreshadowed throughout the entire History.
